algorithms.formula: emissions_tCO2_per_ha = biomass_burned_Mg_per_ha * combustion_completeness * (44/12); biomass_burned ≈ AGB_density * severity_fraction; biome-default AGB densities per IPCC 2019 Refinement Tier-1: tropical_forest 280, temperate_forest 130, boreal_forest 80, savanna 30, grassland 5, cropland 10 (Mg dry biomass / ha); combustion_completeness defaults: forest 0.30, savanna 0.85, grassland 0.95

output.explanation: Per-hectare CO2 emissions proxy from a burned cell, conditioned on biome AGB density and combustion completeness. CH4 and N2O can be added with EF_CH4 = 6.8 g/kg, EF_N2O = 0.20 g/kg dry matter (Andreae 2019).

algorithms.when_to_use: Wildfire MRV / GHG inventory backfill; SDG 13 ghg-proxy reporting; insurance / reinsurance fire claims triage. For full GFED4s parity attach prior AGB and post-fire GPP loss; this is the per-cell first-pass.
algorithms.citation: Andreae, M.O. 2019. Emission of trace gases and aerosols from biomass burning – an updated assessment. Atmos. Chem. Phys. 19:8523–8546. IPCC 2019 Refinement to the 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Vol 4 Ch 2. Key, C.H. & Benson, N.C. 2006. Landscape Assessment (LA) Sampling and Analysis Methods. USDA RMRS-GTR-164-CD.

output.explanation: Bonafoni & Sekertekin 2020 / Liang 2003 narrow-to-broadband shortwave albedo (0.3–3.0 µm) from Sentinel-2 surface reflectance. Used downstream for surface energy balance and radiative-forcing accounting.

algorithms.when_to_use: Per-cell shortwave albedo for radiative-forcing / cool-roofs / snow-cover-loss MRV. Pair with `carbon.albedo_radiative_forcing@1` for the W/m² answer.
algorithms.citation: Liang, S. 2003. Quantitative Remote Sensing of Land Surfaces. Wiley. Bonafoni, S. & Sekertekin, A. 2020. Albedo Retrieval From Sentinel-2 by New Narrow-to-Broadband Conversion Coefficients. IEEE GRSL 17(9):1618–1622.

inputs.explanation: Reference and perturbed albedo are computed via `carbon.surface_albedo_broadband@1` at two epochs (e.g., pre/post intervention).

algorithms.formula: dRF = - S_TOA * T_atm^2 * (alpha_t2 - alpha_t1); S_TOA = 1361 W/m^2; T_atm = 0.854 (clear-sky two-way atmospheric transmittance, Bright 2015)

output.explanation: Local instantaneous shortwave radiative forcing from an albedo change. Positive = surface darkened (warming); negative = brightened (cooling). Convert to CO2-equivalent via Bright 2015 Eq. 7 with global-mean parameters when comparing to carbon credits.

algorithms.when_to_use: Cool-roofs / urban-heat / reforestation co-benefit accounting; surface-albedo carbon offset programmes (VCS VM0042 reforestation albedo footnote).
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/diff {cell, band:'s2.B02', t1, t2} → carbon.surface_albedo_broadband at each epoch → dRF
algorithms.citation: Bright, R.M. 2015. Metrics for biogeophysical climate forcings from land use and land cover changes and their inclusion in life cycle assessment: a critical review. Environmental Science & Technology 49(6):3291–3303. Akbari, H. et al. 2009. Global cooling: increasing world-wide urban albedos to offset CO2. Climatic Change 94:275–286.

inputs.explanation: Long-term JRC GSW v1.4 baseline (1984–2021).

algorithms.formula: p_now = 0.5 * sigmoid(8*indices.mndwi) + 0.5 * sigmoid((-15 - sentinel1_raw)/2); p_hist = surface_water.recurrence / 100; anomaly = p_now - p_hist

output.explanation: Current water-presence probability minus long-term recurrence baseline. Positive = wetter than typical (flood / inundation); negative = drier than typical (drought / drawdown / drainage). Flagged for SDG 6.6.1 reporting.

algorithms.when_to_use: Wetland MRV (Plan Vivo wetland methodology, VM0033 tidal wetlands); drought monitoring of permanent water bodies; SDG 6.6.1 water-related ecosystems indicator.
algorithms.citation: Pekel, J.-F. et al. 2016. High-resolution mapping of global surface water and its long-term changes. Nature 540:418–422. McFeeters 1996; Xu 2006 (MNDWI). UNCCD/UN-Water 2020 SDG 6.6.1 Step-by-Step Methodology.

algorithms.formula: diurnal_amplitude = lst_day_c - lst_night_c; cooling_potential = diurnal_amplitude * (1 - canopy_fraction); higher = more residual heat that additional canopy could sink

output.explanation: Heat residue above what current canopy is removing, proxy for nature-based-cooling investment headroom. Calibrate to °C avoided per ha planted using local empirical work.

algorithms.when_to_use: Urban-cooling carbon credit programmes (Cool City, Trees-for-the-Future urban variants); SDG 11.7 green public spaces siting; municipal heat-action-plan prioritisation.
algorithms.citation: Bowler, D.E. et al. 2010. Urban greening to cool towns and cities: A systematic review of the empirical evidence. Landscape and Urban Planning 97(3):147–155. Akbari, H. & Kolokotsa, D. 2016. Three decades of urban heat islands and mitigation technologies research. Energy and Buildings 133:834–842.

inputs.explanation: Current NDVI sample (S2-derived).
16-day MODIS Terra NDVI as a same-cell prior baseline.
8-vintage 1024-D embedding; trailing-year drop signals structural land-cover change.

algorithms.formula: ndvi_drop = max(0, ndvi_modis_baseline - ndvi_now); embedding_change = 1 - cosine(geotessera.multi_year[year_idx-1], geotessera.multi_year[year_idx]); alert_score = 0.5*clamp01(ndvi_drop / 0.30) + 0.5*clamp01(embedding_change / 0.20)

output.explanation: Composite deforestation/structural-change alert. >0.6 = strong; 0.3–0.6 = candidate (manual review); <0.3 = stable. Calibrate against Hansen GFC v1.10 ground-truth when available; current build is signal-only, not legally binding.

algorithms.when_to_use: Pre-screening for ART-TREES / VCS REDD+ deforestation risk monitoring; plantation guardrails. Confirm with Hansen GFC or Sentinel-2 RGB scene before issuing crediting decisions.
algorithms.citation: Hansen, M.C. et al. 2013. High-resolution global maps of 21st-century forest cover change. Science 342:850–853. Verra VT0007 (Unplanned Deforestation Allocation Tool, 2023). Reiche, J. et al. 2018. Combining satellite data for better tropical forest monitoring. Nature Climate Change 8:1119–1124.

inputs.explanation: EVI→FPAR linear, Sims 2006 + Xiao 2004; intercept 0.10, slope 1.16, clamped to [0,1].
Photosynthetically active radiation ≈ 45% of shortwave (Britton & Dodd 1976).
Light-use-efficiency temperature scalar; 0–30°C optimum (CASA model).

algorithms.formula: npp_proxy = LUE_max * fpar_proxy * par_proxy * lue_temp_scalar; LUE_max = 0.55 g_C / MJ (biome-mean default; biome-specific overrides per Potter 1993)

output.explanation: CASA-style light-use-efficiency NPP at 10 m S2 grain, useful where MODIS 500 m is too coarse (smallholder agroforestry, urban canopy, mangroves). Calibrate LUE against MOD17 over the same cell to remove bias.

algorithms.when_to_use: High-resolution (10 m) NPP for smallholder, urban, or coastal carbon programmes where MOD17 misses sub-pixel structure. Pair with `carbon.npp_8day@1` for cross-check.
algorithms.citation: Potter, C.S. et al. 1993. Terrestrial ecosystem production: a process model based on global satellite and surface data. Global Biogeochemical Cycles 7(4):811–841. Sims, D.A. et al. 2006. On the use of MODIS EVI to assess gross primary productivity of North American ecosystems. JGR 111:G04015. Xiao, X. et al. 2004. Modeling gross primary production of temperate deciduous broadleaf forest using satellite images. Remote Sensing of Environment 91:256–270.

inputs.explanation: Current 8-day GPP for context.
30-day cumulative precipitation deficit (mm/day mean); clamp at 3 mm/day saturation.

algorithms.formula: stress = 0.40*moisture + 0.30*thermal_stress + 0.30*precip_30d; carbon_loss_proxy = stress * modis.gpp_8day

output.explanation: Stress-weighted GPP-at-risk. High = drought is actively suppressing carbon uptake; agents reporting credits should disclose stress > 0.6 windows as material to baseline integrity.

algorithms.when_to_use: REDD+ / ARR project integrity monitoring during drought; insurance parametric trigger design. Surface stress component breakdown in the receipt, moisture vs thermal vs precipitation deficit drives different management responses.
algorithms.citation: Vicente-Serrano, S.M. et al. 2010. A multiscalar drought index sensitive to global warming: the SPEI. Journal of Climate 23(7):1696–1718. Ciais, P. et al. 2005. Europe-wide reduction in primary productivity caused by the heat and drought in 2003. Nature 437:529–533.

algorithms.formula: p_now = 0.5*sigmoid(8*indices.mndwi) + 0.5*sigmoid((-15 - sentinel1_raw)/2); p_hist = surface_water.recurrence/100; beta = (p_now - p_hist) / max(p_hist, 0.05); class = if beta > 0.10 -> 'increase' else if beta < -0.10 -> 'decrease' else 'stable'

output.explanation: SDG 6.6.1 indicator (change in extent of water-related ecosystems over time). beta is the relative change against the 1984–2021 JRC baseline; ±10% threshold per UN-Water 2020 step-by-step methodology.

algorithms.when_to_use: National voluntary review reporting against SDG 6.6.1; basin-scale water-ecosystem change. Aggregate over the basin polygon for the headline indicator.
algorithms.citation: UN-Water 2020. SDG 6.6.1 Step-by-Step Methodology. Pekel, J.-F. et al. 2016. Nature 540:418–422.

inputs.explanation: 16-day MODIS NDVI series across the reporting window (default 2015→present per Trends.Earth).

algorithms.formula: fit OLS slope on annual NDVI sums; mann_kendall_p = MK test; class = if slope > 0 and p < 0.05 -> 'increase' else if slope < 0 and p < 0.05 -> 'decrease' else if slope < 0 and p >= 0.05 -> 'early_signs_of_decline' else if abs(slope) < 0.001 -> 'stable_not_stressed' else 'stable_but_stressed'

output.explanation: Sub-indicator 1 of SDG 15.3.1 (Trends.Earth land productivity dynamics). Combines OLS slope significance with stress flag (NDVI sum < threshold).

algorithms.when_to_use: Land-degradation-neutrality (LDN) reporting against UNCCD SDG 15.3.1. Pair with land-cover-change and SOC sub-indicators for the full proportion-of-degraded-land headline.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {cell, band:'modis.ndvi_mean', window:[2015-01-01, t]} → MK + OLS
algorithms.citation: UNCCD 2017. Default Methodology for Sub-Indicator 15.3.1. Conservation International 2018. Trends.Earth: Productivity. Hamed & Rao 1998. Modified MK test. Journal of Hydrology 204:182–196.

algorithms.formula: is_mountain = (elevation >= 2500) OR (elevation >= 1500 AND local_slope >= 2 deg) OR (elevation >= 1000 AND local_slope >= 5 deg) OR (elevation >= 300 AND local_relief_7km > 300); kapos_class follows; is_green = indices.ndvi >= 0.30; MGCI = (sum_polygon(is_mountain AND is_green) / sum_polygon(is_mountain)) * 100

output.explanation: FAO SDG 15.4.2 Mountain Green Cover Index, share of mountain area with NDVI ≥ 0.30 vegetation cover. Higher = healthier mountain ecosystem. Slope and relief computed from copdem30m using a 7-km neighbourhood (FAO 2020 methodology).

algorithms.when_to_use: National voluntary review reporting on SDG 15.4.2; mountain-region restoration prioritisation.
algorithms.citation: FAO 2020. Mountain Green Cover Index, SDG indicator 15.4.2 metadata. Kapos, V. et al. 2000. Developing a map of the world's mountain forests. In Forests in Sustainable Mountain Development.

inputs.explanation: Hourly PM2.5 across the reporting calendar year.

algorithms.formula: annual_mean = mean(pm25 over 8760 hourly samples in calendar year); flag = if annual_mean <= 5.0 -> 'meets_WHO_2021_AQG' else if <= 10 -> 'WHO_IT-4' else if <= 15 -> 'WHO_IT-3' else if <= 25 -> 'WHO_IT-2' else if <= 35 -> 'WHO_IT-1' else 'exceeds_WHO_IT-1'

output.explanation: SDG 11.6.2 annual mean PM2.5 with WHO 2021 Air Quality Guideline / Interim Target classification (5/10/15/25/35 µg/m³ ladder). Aggregate over city polygon for the headline.

algorithms.when_to_use: City SDG 11.6.2 reporting and air-quality scorecards. Backfill via /v1/backfill on cams.pm25 then aggregate.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/backfill {cell, band:'cams.pm25', from, to} → mean → classify
algorithms.citation: WHO 2021. WHO global air quality guidelines: PM2.5/PM10/O3/NO2/SO2/CO. UNEP 2022 SDG 11.6.2 metadata. van Donkelaar, A. et al. 2021. Monthly Global Estimates of Fine Particulate Matter. Environ. Sci. Technol. 55:15287–15300.

inputs.explanation: FAI > 0.02 indicates floating algal aggregations (Hu 2009).
Surrogate for surface chlorophyll under bloom conditions (Mishra & Mishra 2012).

algorithms.formula: bloom_signal = clamp01(indices.fai / 0.05) * 0.50 + clamp01((indices.gndvi - 0.10) / 0.40) * 0.30 + clamp01(indices.tss) * 0.20; sst_modifier = sigmoid((marine.sst - 24)/2); cei = bloom_signal * sst_modifier

output.explanation: SDG 14.1.1a coastal eutrophication proxy. > 0.6 = anomalous bloom signal warranting confirmation; SST modifier suppresses spurious cool-water alerts.

algorithms.when_to_use: Coastal water-quality screening; SDG 14.1.1a national reporting first-pass. Confirm with in-situ chlorophyll-a or Sentinel-3 OLCI Chl-a (when materialised) before declaring an indicator-grade bloom.
algorithms.citation: Hu, C. 2009. A novel ocean color index to detect floating algae in the global oceans. Remote Sensing of Environment 113(10):2118–2129. Mishra, S. & Mishra, D.R. 2012. Normalized difference chlorophyll index: A novel model for remote estimation of chlorophyll-a concentration in turbid productive waters. RSE 117:394–406. UNEP 2018. SDG 14.1.1a metadata.

algorithms.formula: see carbon.fire_emissions_co2_proxy@1; aggregate per-cell tCO2_per_ha to national totals; report as Gg CO2-eq (multiply by 1e-3) for SDG 13.2.2 'total greenhouse gas emissions per year' biomass-burning sub-component

output.explanation: SDG 13.2.2 biomass-burning sub-component proxy. Sum over national polygon and convert to Gg CO2-eq.

algorithms.when_to_use: National greenhouse-gas inventory backfill where ground-truth fire activity data is incomplete; complements UNFCCC Biennial Update Reports.
algorithms.citation: UNFCCC SDG 13.2.2 metadata. IPCC 2019 Refinement Vol 4 Ch 2. GFED4s methodology, van der Werf et al. 2017. Earth System Science Data 9:697–720.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-1 RTC γ⁰ VV in dB. Soil moisture relates monotonically to backscatter once vegetation and roughness effects are removed.
Used only to gate steep-slope cells (slope > 15°) where SAR backscatter is dominated by topography, not moisture.
Dense canopy (NDVI > 0.6) attenuates the soil-surface signal — the algorithm reports lower confidence in that regime.
Recent rainfall raises the prior on high SM and is used to flag inversion-failure cells.

algorithms.formula: let s = sentinel1_raw_db; if NDVI > 0.6 -> confidence:'low'; sm_pct = clamp(((s - sigma0_dry) / (sigma0_wet - sigma0_dry)) * 100, 0, 100) where sigma0_dry, sigma0_wet are per-cell long-term percentiles (5th, 95th) over a 24-month sentinel1 history; SSM_TUWien-style change-detection

output.explanation: Surface (top ~5 cm) volumetric soil moisture saturation [0,100]%. Calibrated per cell against its own long-term γ⁰ envelope (TU Wien change-detection paradigm). NOT a direct VWC measurement; pair with `weather.precipitation_mm` for sanity. Reports `confidence:'low'` when NDVI > 0.6 (canopy-blocked) or slope > 15° (terrain-dominated). For root-zone moisture, use a soil-water-balance model on top of this surface estimate.

algorithms.when_to_use: All-weather plot soil-moisture under cloud (monsoon, winter fog). Do NOT use on dense forest, urban cores, or steep slopes — `indices.ndmi` is the right choice for canopy water content. Calibrate against in-situ probes on ~5% of plots if you want to claim absolute VWC.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {band:'sentinel1_raw', from:t-24mo, to:t} → percentile envelope; POST /v1/recall {bands:['sentinel1_raw','indices.ndvi','copdem30m.elevation_mean']} → apply formula
algorithms.citation: Wagner, W., Lemoine, G. & Rott, H. 1999. A method for estimating soil moisture from ERS scatterometer and soil data. Remote Sensing of Environment 70:191–207. Bauer-Marschallinger, B. et al. 2018. Toward global soil moisture monitoring with Sentinel-1: harmonising assets and analysing data with TU Wien algorithm. Remote Sensing of Environment 213:1–18.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: every Sentinel-1 revisit (~6 d in Europe / ~12 d elsewhere). Trajectory window of 24 mo gives a stable percentile envelope; rebuild every quarter.
algorithms.accuracy_band: R² 0.50–0.70 vs in-situ probes on bare/sparse fields (Bauer-Marschallinger 2018). Drops to R² ~0.3 under dense canopy — surface as `confidence:'low'`.

inputs.explanation: Bare-soil index — SOC retrieval works only on visible bare soil; vegetation hides the SOC absorption features.
Sentinel-2 SWIR1 (1610 nm) — clay/Fe-oxide absorption window, partial SOC contribution.
Sentinel-2 SWIR2 (2190 nm) — strongest single-band SOC predictor in S2 (Vaudour et al. 2022).
Wet soil reflectance suppresses SWIR — algorithm masks NDMI > 0.2 or applies a moisture-correction term.
Elevation + slope are top-3 covariates in SoilGrids 2.0 random-forest (Hengl et al. 2017).

ISRIC SoilGrids 2.0 global prior at 250 m, 0–30 cm thickness-weighted topsoil mean (g/kg). The algorithm Bayesian-blends this with the S2-derived residual when both are available; falls back to prior-only if S2 is cloud-blocked.

algorithms.formula: if NDMI > 0.2 or BSI < 0.05 -> tier:'A_prior_only'; soc_est = soilgrids.soc_0_30cm; else { ndvi_bare_score = clamp01((bsi - 0.05)/0.20); s2_residual = α0 + α1*B11 + α2*B12 + α3*elevation; soc_est = (1-w)*soilgrids.soc_0_30cm + w*(soilgrids.soc_0_30cm + s2_residual) where w = 0.5 * ndvi_bare_score; tier:'B_s2_blend' }; tier:'C_lab_calibrated' is reserved for receipts that cite a lab-measurement attestation_cid

output.explanation: Topsoil (0–30 cm) Soil Organic Carbon concentration in g C / kg soil. Three-tier confidence label (A_prior_only / B_s2_blend / C_lab_calibrated) MUST be surfaced — DO NOT use tier-B for carbon-credit certification. Tier-A R² ~0.4 (global), tier-B R² ~0.65 (in-region with calibration), tier-C R² > 0.85 (lab samples). Multiply by bulk_density × depth_m × 100 to convert g/kg → t SOC / ha.

algorithms.when_to_use: Plot-level SOC screening, agronomic carbon stocktake, baseline for credit projects (tier-C ONLY). Tier-B is suitable for change-detection between two well-separated dates (≥1 yr) but NOT for absolute claims at certification grade.
algorithms.citation: Hengl, T. et al. 2017. SoilGrids250m: Global gridded soil information based on machine learning. PLoS ONE 12(2):e0169748. Castaldi, F. et al. 2018. Mapping SOC at European scale by integrating Sentinel-2 imagery with European LUCAS data. Geoderma 327:153–165. Vaudour, E. et al. 2022. Sentinel-2 image capacities to predict common topsoil properties of temperate and Mediterranean agro-ecosystems. RSE 273:112978. Stevens, A. et al. 2008. Laboratory, field and airborne spectroscopy for monitoring SOC: state-of-the-art. Geoderma 144:395–404.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: annual (post-harvest, bare-soil window). Sub-annual updates ARE NOT defensible — SOC stocks change <1%/yr in undisturbed soils; instrument noise dominates inside that interval.
algorithms.accuracy_band: tier-A R² ~0.40 (global SoilGrids prior alone); tier-B R² 0.55–0.70 (S2 + DEM blended, in-region); tier-C R² > 0.85 (lab-calibrated). NEVER advertise tier-B at tier-C accuracy.

inputs.explanation: soc_estimate_s2_dem@1 evaluated at baseline date t0 (project crediting period start).
soc_estimate_s2_dem@1 evaluated at monitoring date t1; (t1 - t0) MUST be ≥ 1 yr per VM0042 v2.0.
Used to infer the depth fraction the topsoil concentration applies to; default 0–30 cm consistent with VM0042 standard depth.

algorithms.formula: if (t1 - t0) < 1.0 yr -> reject:'insufficient_separation'; tCO2e_per_ha = (soc_t1 - soc_t0)[g/kg] × bulk_density[1.3 t/m³ default] × depth[0.30 m] × 100[ha→m² scaler] × (44/12)[CO₂/C molar ratio] × buffer_pool_discount[default 0.20] × uncertainty_discount[VCS Tool VT0006 deduction at the t0/t1 confidence tier]

output.explanation: Issuable VM0042 v2.0 soil-carbon credit per hectare, after IPCC stoichiometric conversion (44/12 CO₂/C), default 20% buffer pool deduction (VCS AFOLU non-permanence buffer), and a confidence-tier-driven uncertainty discount. NEGATIVE values mean SOC loss = a credit DEBIT, not a credit. NEVER compute on tier-A or tier-B inputs without a calibration receipt — the result has no defensible confidence interval.

algorithms.when_to_use: Voluntary soil-carbon project MRV (Verra VM0042, Gold Standard SustainCERT, CAR Soil Enrichment Protocol). The receipt MUST cite both soc_t0 and soc_t1 fact_cids AND a lab-calibration attestation_cid for tier-C use; Verra auditors will reject otherwise.
algorithms.primitive: soc_estimate_s2_dem@1 at t0 → soc_t0; soc_estimate_s2_dem@1 at t1 → soc_t1; apply formula. For polygon aggregation use /v1/recall_polygon at both dates.
algorithms.citation: Verra Verified Carbon Standard. 2023. VM0042 — Methodology for Improved Agricultural Land Management v2.0. IPCC 2019 Refinement to the 2006 Guidelines for National GHG Inventories, Vol 4 Ch 5 (Soil Carbon). Smith, P. et al. 2020. How to measure, report and verify soil carbon change. Global Change Biology 26:219–241. Paustian, K. et al. 2019. Soil C sequestration as a biological negative emission strategy. Frontiers in Climate 1:8.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: annual, anchored to project crediting period (typically t0 + Nyr where N ∈ [5,40]). Cannot be claimed sub-annually — VM0042 v2.0 §6.2 requires ≥1 yr separation between baseline and monitoring measurements.
algorithms.accuracy_band: uncertainty discount per VCS Tool VT0006: tier-A: 50% deduction, tier-B: 30% deduction, tier-C: 10% deduction. Surface BOTH the gross ΔSOC and the net issuable credit so auditors can verify the discount.

inputs.explanation: Z-score of current NDVI vs the cell's long-term mean for the same DOY ±15 d (multi-year baseline). Negative anomaly = generic stress flag.
Negative NDMI anomaly correlates with water stress — the discriminator for `water` vs other stress types.
NDRE drops 1–2 weeks before NDVI under N deficiency (Gitelson 2003). The discriminator for `nitrogen` stress.
Crop heat-tolerance: lst_day_8day > crop-specific threshold drives the `heat` attribution.

algorithms.formula: z_ndvi  = (ndvi - μ_ndvi)  / σ_ndvi;  z_ndmi = (ndmi - μ_ndmi)/σ_ndmi; z_ndre = (ndre - μ_ndre)/σ_ndre; z_lst = (lst - μ_lst)/σ_lst; if z_ndvi >= -0.5 -> 'healthy'; else attribute = argmax over { 'water': clamp01(-z_ndmi), 'nitrogen': clamp01(-z_ndre - max(0,-z_ndmi)), 'heat': clamp01(z_lst), 'unexplained': 0.5 } subject to argmax_score >= 0.3 else 'unexplained'

output.explanation: Single dominant stress type per cell. `unexplained` is a deliberate routing signal — it should trigger a field-officer photo + CV pathogen ID rather than be hidden behind a generic 'stress' flag. Confidence is the gap between the top-1 and top-2 attribution scores; surface < 0.15 as low-confidence.

algorithms.when_to_use: Action-taking workflows where the type drives the recommendation (irrigate vs fertilise vs heat-stress mitigation vs scout for disease). REQUIRES per-cell, per-crop, per-phenology-stage z-score baselines built from at least 3 years of NASA POWER + Sentinel-2 history; without that calibration the attribution collapses to noise.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory for ndvi/ndmi/ndre/lst over 3-yr DOY±15d window → μ,σ; POST /v1/recall current bands; apply formula
algorithms.citation: Sandholt, I., Rasmussen, K. & Andersen, J. 2002. A simple interpretation of the surface temperature/vegetation index space for assessment of surface moisture status. RSE 79:213–224. Gitelson, A.A., Gritz, Y. & Merzlyak, M.N. 2003. Relationships between leaf chlorophyll content and spectral reflectance. Journal of Plant Physiology 160:271–282. Anderegg, W.R.L. et al. 2019. Climate-driven risks to forest carbon. Science 368:1327.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: every Sentinel-2 cloud-free observation (~5–10 d non-monsoon, weekly composite during monsoon with SAR-fill). Stress classification updates faster than yield models because field response windows are <2 weeks.
algorithms.accuracy_band: 70–90% precision per stress class once per-crop baselines exist (typed precision of `unexplained` is by definition a referral signal, not a measurement).

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 NDRE = (B8A − B5)/(B8A + B5). Red-edge absorption tracks chlorophyll/N concentration in canopy leaves.
MODIS Leaf Area Index 500 m. Coarse for sub-half-acre plots — fall back to a Sentinel-2 LAI head when materialised.
Gate: NDVI < 0.3 means no canopy yet — N-uptake is meaningless on bare soil; algorithm returns Absence in that regime.

algorithms.formula: if NDVI < 0.3 -> Absence{reason:'pre_canopy'}; N_concentration_pct = clamp(0.50 + 4.50 * NDRE, 1.0, 5.5);  N_uptake_kg_ha = N_concentration_pct * 0.01 * (LAI * specific_leaf_mass_g_m2[default 50]) * 10  // kg/ha = mass(g/m²) × area(m²/ha) × 1e-1; default specific_leaf_mass per crop class

output.explanation: ABOVE-GROUND NITROGEN UPTAKE — the kg N / ha currently held in standing plant tissue at the time of observation. THIS IS NOT FERTILIZER APPLICATION RATE. The output answers 'how much N is in the canopy now?' (a remote-sensing-derived plant-status variable), NOT 'how much fertiliser has been applied?' (an input-rate variable that needs a separate input map). Driver for top-dressing recommendations and mid-season N-deficit alerting. Calibrate `specific_leaf_mass` per crop and per phenology stage; the SPAD-leaf-tissue ground-truth set is what closes the loop. Output of <30 kg N/ha mid-season on a high-input crop = strong N-deficit signal.

algorithms.when_to_use: Mid-season N-deficit detection on cereals (wheat, rice, maize) and high-N horticulture. Pair with NDRE temporal trajectory (drop > 0.05 in 7 d = active deficit) to surface 'apply N within 7 days' recommendations. NOT for legumes — they fix atmospheric N and the canopy-N relationship inverts. NOT a substitute for fertilizer-application-rate input maps in N2O emission accounting.
algorithms.citation: Gitelson, A.A., Gritz, Y. & Merzlyak, M.N. 2003. Relationships between leaf chlorophyll content and spectral reflectance. Journal of Plant Physiology 160:271–282. Cao, Q. et al. 2017. Active canopy sensing of winter wheat nitrogen status: an evaluation of two sensors, NDRE-based VIs, and a soil reflectance NIR-based VI. Field Crops Research 154:133–144. Clevers, J.G.P.W. & Kooistra, L. 2012. Using hyperspectral remote sensing data for retrieving canopy chlorophyll and nitrogen content. IEEE JSTARS 5:574–583. For the orthogonal fertilizer-input-rate map see Adalibieke, W. et al. 2023. Global crop-specific nitrogen fertilization dataset (1961–2020). Scientific Data 10:617. DOI 10.1038/s41597-023-02526-z. For HYDE 3.4 crop-N gridded inputs see Klein Goldewijk, K. et al. 2017. HYDE 3.2 (with 3.4 update 2024). Earth System Science Data 9:927.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: every Sentinel-2 cloud-free pass (5-day revisit cadence, ~8–14 d cloud-permitting in non-monsoon). Sub-weekly updates not informative — leaf-N changes on ~3-day timescale at most.
algorithms.accuracy_band: NDRE→leaf-N R²~0.77 (Gitelson 2003); NDRE×LAI→kg N/ha (above-ground plant uptake) R²~0.85 with crop-specific SPAD calibration on ≥200 plots (Cao et al. 2017); without SPAD calibration treat as relative ranking only, not absolute kg/ha. The output is plant-tissue N — it does NOT estimate fertiliser-input rate, and its R² against measured fertiliser input is approximately zero (it confounds soil supply, mineralisation, atmospheric deposition, and applied fertiliser into a single canopy signal).
algorithms.pitfalls: 1. DO NOT use this as a proxy for fertilizer-application rate. The output is canopy-resident plant N (above-ground uptake), which is the integral of soil supply + mineralisation + atmospheric deposition + applied fertiliser minus losses (leaching, volatilisation, denitrification) — NOT the application input. For fertiliser-input-rate maps use a dedicated input dataset: Adalibieke et al. 2023 (Sci Data 10:617, crop-specific N at 5 arc-min globally), HYDE 3.4 crop-N gridded inputs, or the FAO FertiSTAT national-statistics product. 2. DO NOT use this output directly for N2O emission estimation. N2O accounting requires the fertiliser-input map AND the IPCC 2019 Refinement Vol 4 Ch 11 EF1 default (0.010 kg N2O-N per kg N input, with regional refinements for rain-fed vs irrigated) — combine the fertiliser map with EF1, not this uptake signal. 3. Specific-leaf-mass defaults to 50 g/m² (generic) and is the largest source of error; calibrate per crop and per phenology stage (wheat early-tillering ≈ 35, wheat anthesis ≈ 65, maize V6 ≈ 40, maize V12 ≈ 55) for any operational use. 4. NDVI gate (NDVI < 0.3 → Absence) prevents bare-soil noise but also clips legitimate post-anthesis senescence — flag explicitly in growth-stage-aware applications. 5. NOT for legumes (soybean, pulses) — atmospheric-N fixation breaks the chlorophyll–uptake relationship and over-predicts uptake by 1.5–2× in nodulated stands.

inputs.explanation: JRC Global Forest Cover for the year 2020 V3 — the EU Commission's expected (non-binding) baseline for EUDR Due Diligence Statements. Value 1 means the cell met the Regulation 2023/1115 Article 2(4) forest definition at the 2020-12-31 cut-off date: >0.5 ha, >5 m tree height, >10 % canopy cover, excluding land predominantly under agricultural or urban use.

Hansen GFC v1.12 baseline tree-cover percentage at year 2000. Used as a confirming baseline at the 10 % EUDR threshold per Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 Article 2(4) — NOT the 30 % canopy threshold from FAO 2020 Global Forest Resources Assessment, which is what an earlier vintage of this algorithm key (removed 2026-05-17) used incorrectly.

Year of the FIRST Hansen-detected tree-cover loss event. The Hansen materialiser at this responder decodes the raw byte to the calendar year (2001..2024 in v1.12); 0 means 'no loss observed in coverage'. Value >= 2021 means loss after the EUDR cut-off date of 31 December 2020.

algorithms.formula: let forest_at_cutoff = (jrc_gfc2020.forest_2020 >= 1) OR (forest_change.treecover2000 >= 10); if NOT forest_at_cutoff -> 'not_in_scope' [code 3]; else if forest_change.lossyear >= 2021 -> 'fail' [code 2]; else -> 'pass' [code 1]. (forest_change.lossyear is the calendar year as decoded by the Hansen v1.12 materialiser; 0 = no loss observed.) Optional refinement layer (handled at /v1/eudr_dds): if wri_gdm.driver_class is available and in {5=wildfire, 7=natural} -> reclassify 'fail' -> 'not_in_scope'; if radd.alert_date is available and post-2020-12-31 -> upgrade 'pass' -> 'fail' for cells where Hansen optical was cloud-blocked. Verdict 'indeterminate' [code 4] is emitted by the dispatcher when any required input is missing.

output.explanation: EUDR per-cell compliance verdict. 1 = pass (forest at 2020-12-31 cut-off, no Hansen loss after cut-off). 2 = fail (forest at cut-off, Hansen loss in 2021 or later — pair with wri_gdm.driver_class to confirm commodity-driven). 3 = not_in_scope (cell did not meet the EUDR forest definition at the cut-off date — neither JRC GFC2020 V3 forest nor Hansen ≥10 % canopy). 4 = indeterminate (a required input was Absence; dispatcher emits the indeterminate label out-of-band, AST cannot itself emit 4).

algorithms.when_to_use: EUDR Due Diligence Statements for the seven controlled commodity groups (cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soy, wood and their derivatives) under Regulation (EU) 2023/1115. Pair with the /v1/eudr_dds endpoint for the polygon-aggregated Annex II-shaped output; the per-cell algorithm here is the unit primitive.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall_polygon {polygon, bands:['jrc_gfc2020.forest_2020','forest_change.treecover2000','forest_change.lossyear']} → apply Expr AST per cell → aggregate. Or POST /v1/eudr_dds for the full Annex II envelope.
algorithms.citation: European Union. 2023. Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 of 31 May 2023 on the making available on the Union market and the export from the Union of certain commodities and products associated with deforestation and forest degradation (Article 2(4) forest definition, Article 2(28) geolocation rule, Article 8 due diligence, Article 9 + Annex II DDS). Bourgoin, C. et al. 2026. GFC2020: A Global Map of Forest Land Use for year 2020 to support the EU Deforestation Regulation. ESSD 18:1331. DOI 10.5194/essd-2025-351. Hansen, M.C. et al. 2013. High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change. Science 342:850–853 (annual updates; v1.12 = 2025 release covering loss through 2024). Sims, M.J. et al. 2025. Global drivers of forest loss at 1 km resolution. Environmental Research Letters 20:074027.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: annual at minimum (Hansen GFC publishes a new vintage each year, typically April). For active commodity supply chains, re-run on every Hansen v1.x release AND on every RADD/GLAD near-real-time alert to detect new losses inside the EUDR cut-off window. JRC GFC2020 is a single 2020 baseline; it does not need annual recall.
algorithms.accuracy_band: JRC GFC2020 V3 producer's accuracy 88 % globally, 80-85 % in the agroforestry-dominated West African belt (Bourgoin et al. 2026). Hansen GFC user's accuracy 87 % for 2000 cover ≥ 25 %, 73 % for cover 0-25 % (Hansen et al. 2013, supplementary). Compound expected DDS-grade accuracy with WRI GDM driver-attribution filter on top: ~95 % for the 'fail' verdict when both baselines and the driver class agree.
algorithms.honest_limits: 1. EUDR explicitly excludes land 'predominantly under agricultural or urban land use' (Article 2(4)) — this algorithm does NOT detect that exclusion at the cell level; agroforestry plots (shade-grown coffee, cocoa, rubber plantations) may misregister. Pair with wri_gdm.driver_class at the /v1/eudr_dds layer for commodity-driven loss attribution. 2. JRC GFC2020 is labelled by the EU 'non-mandatory, non-exclusive and not legally binding' — the Commission funded it specifically to support EUDR audits, but operators may use other defensible forest baselines. 3. This algorithm covers the geolocation+deforestation parts of the DDS only; Article 9(1)(b) legality verification (country-of-origin laws, land tenure, indigenous community consent under FPIC) is structurally out of Earth-observation scope. 4. The 4-hectare POINT-vs-POLYGON dispatch rule of Article 2(28) is handled at the /v1/eudr_dds endpoint, not in this per-cell algorithm.

inputs.explanation: JRC GFC2020 V3 forest definition baseline.
Hansen canopy at year 2000; 10 % threshold for EUDR confirmation.
Hansen first-loss year; >= 21 (2021+) = post-cut-off.
RADD Sentinel-1 SAR-based alert date — orchestrated fallback for cells where Sentinel-2 optical is cloud-blocked. Humid-tropics-only (50 countries).
Sims et al. 2025 driver attribution (1 km). Reclassifies wildfire (class 5) and other natural disturbance (class 7) loss out of EUDR scope.

algorithms.formula: Polygon-in, signed-Annex-II-DDS-out wrapper around eudr_compliance@1. Steps: (1) Read the input polygon; if any plot has area <= 4 ha treat the centroid as a POINT per Article 2(28), else a POLYGON. (2) For each cell inside the plot, evaluate eudr_compliance@1's AST. (3) Layer wri_gdm.driver_class refinement: cells with driver_class in {5,7} downgrade 'fail' -> 'not_in_scope'. (4) Layer radd.alert_date orchestration: cells where Hansen reports no loss but RADD reports a post-cut-off alert with confidence >= 3 upgrade 'pass' -> 'fail'. (5) Aggregate: polygon verdict = FAIL if any cell is 'fail' AND failing-cell area / total plot area >= 0.001 (0.1 % threshold; smaller footprints surface as 'fail_below_de_minimis' to flag edge-effect false positives). (6) Emit the Annex II structured envelope with the signed receipt: every cell's fact_cid is cited; the polygon-level verdict carries its own algorithm_cid.

output.explanation: EUDR Due Diligence Statement polygon-level verdict, per Annex II of Regulation 2023/1115. The /v1/eudr_dds endpoint emits the full structured envelope (operator, commodity, HS/CN, country of production, geolocation block per Article 2(28), risk_assessment_outcome). This algorithm key is the metadata anchor; the actual aggregation logic lives in the endpoint handler because the per-cell AST cannot model polygon aggregation.

algorithms.when_to_use: Producing a TRACES-NT-uploadable DDS for an EU operator placing relevant commodities on the Union market under Regulation 2023/1115. Always paired with the operator's own commodity + supplier metadata (HS/CN code, country of production, quantity) which the endpoint accepts as request fields. Never run for legality verification — Article 9(1)(b) legality is structurally out of Earth-observation scope.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/eudr_dds {polygons:[{plot_id, geometry_geojson, country_of_production, commodity_hs, quantity_kg, operator}], cut_off_date:'2020-12-31'} → Annex II envelope with per-plot verdict + signed cell receipts.
algorithms.citation: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 of 31 May 2023, Articles 2(4), 2(28), 8, 9 and Annex II. Commission Guidance C(2024)/04524. EUDR Information System: https://eudr.webcloud.ec.europa.eu/tracesnt/login (TRACES NT API spec lives in CircaBC; SOAP/XML behind EU Login).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: per DDS submission; an operator typically runs once before placing a consignment on the Union market and stores the signed receipt as part of the company's due-diligence record (Article 12 record-keeping; 5 years).
algorithms.accuracy_band: Inherits the per-cell accuracy of eudr_compliance@1 (~88-95 % depending on biome and driver-class refinement) and adds polygon-level certainty bounds: the endpoint emits failing-cell area, polygon area, and failure_fraction so the operator can show defensible numbers in a contested DDS.
algorithms.honest_limits: 1. NOT a substitute for Article 9(1)(b) legality verification (land tenure, FPIC, country-of-origin law compliance) — legality requires a partner module. The DDS envelope carries a legality_module: 'none' field by default to make this explicit to downstream operators. 2. JRC GFC2020 V3 is the EU Commission's expected (not legally binding) forest baseline. Operators choosing a different baseline must document the choice; the DDS envelope accepts a forest_baseline_override field. 3. The 4-hectare Article 2(28) POINT-vs-POLYGON dispatch is implemented per-plot; cattle plots are POLYGON regardless of size per the regulation's cattle exemption. 4. TRACES NT submission is the operator's responsibility; this endpoint produces the structured payload but does not authenticate to the EU Information System on the operator's behalf.

inputs.explanation: Pre-vs-post Normalized Burn Ratio drop ≥ 0.20 = burn-severity signature (Key & Benson 2006).
Pre-vs-post Normalized Difference Tillage Index drop = residue removal/destruction signal (van Deventer 1997).
MODIS MCD64A1 monthly burned-area boolean, 500 m. Independent confirmation.
CAMS aerosol optical depth at 550 nm — smoke plumes spike AOD by ≥0.30 over baseline (Chen et al. 2017).
Surface PM₂.₅ spike — anchors public-health framing for state pollution boards.

algorithms.formula: let s_nbr = step(dnbr >= 0.20); s_ndti = step(dndti >= 0.10); s_modis = step(modis.burned_area_monthly >= 0.5); s_aod = step(cams.aod_550 >= baseline + 0.30); s_pm25 = step(cams.pm25 >= baseline + 50 ug/m³); agreement = s_nbr + s_ndti + s_modis + s_aod + s_pm25; verdict = if agreement >= 3 -> 'high_confidence_burn'; else if agreement == 2 -> 'likely_burn'; else if agreement == 1 -> 'single_sensor_signal'; else 'no_burn'

output.explanation: Multi-sensor burn-detection consensus, designed for forensic-grade evidence (>95% precision when 3+ sensors agree per Punjab/Haryana paddy-burn validation studies). The receipt cites all five fact_cids; agencies (Punjab Pollution Control Board, NABARD residue-management subsidies) accept this as legal evidence. NDTI temporal pattern is what separates BURN from MULCH/TILLAGE — surface that distinction in the explanation.

algorithms.when_to_use: Post-harvest residue-burn enforcement (paddy in Punjab/Haryana Oct–Nov, wheat in Mar–Apr); state air-quality dashboards; insurance subrogation for downstream PM₂.₅-related claims. Always pair with VIIRS active fire (when wired) for sub-day detection — MODIS burned-area is monthly composite, lags by up to 30 d.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory for indices.nbr, indices.ndti over a 14-day window straddling the suspected burn date; POST /v1/recall {bands:['modis.burned_area_monthly','cams.aod_550','cams.pm25']}; apply formula
algorithms.citation: Key, C.H. & Benson, N.C. 2006. Landscape Assessment: Sampling and Analysis Methods. USDA-FS RMRS-GTR-164. van Deventer, A.P. et al. 1997. Using thematic mapper data to identify contrasting soil plains and tillage practices. PE&RS 63(1):87–93. Giglio, L. et al. 2018. The Collection 6 MODIS burned area mapping algorithm and product. RSE 217:72–85. Liu, T. et al. 2019. Crop residue burning practices across north India inferred from household survey data: bridging gaps in satellite observations. Atmospheric Environment 6:100091.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: every Sentinel-2 pass during burn season (Oct–Nov, Mar–Apr in north-Indian Indo-Gangetic plain). Outside burn season, weekly suffices. For NRT enforcement, drop in VIIRS active-fire (375 m, ~3 hr latency) and run hourly.
algorithms.accuracy_band: >95% precision for `high_confidence_burn` (≥3-sensor agreement) in validation studies of Punjab/Haryana paddy stubble. `likely_burn` ~85% precision; `single_sensor_signal` ~60% — useful as a triage/scout signal only.

inputs.explanation: NDWI > 0.0 across rice-canopy phenology = continuous flooding signal; oscillation = AWD (Alternate Wetting and Drying).
Sentinel-1 γ⁰ VV < -17 dB under canopy = standing-water signature (Bouvet et al. 2018) — all-weather confirmation under monsoon cloud.
Methanogen activity scales with paddy-water temperature (Q10 ≈ 2.5); IPCC 2019 EF baseline is at 25 °C.
Cumulative NDVI over the season scales with rice biomass and substrate availability for methanogens.

algorithms.formula: Step 1 (regime classification from NDWI trajectory across season): if mean(ndwi) > 0.0 AND oscillation_count(ndwi crossing 0.0) <= 1 -> 'continuous_flood'; else if oscillation_count >= 3 -> 'AWD' (Alternate Wetting and Drying, multiple aeration cycles); else if mean(ndwi) > 0.0 AND oscillation_count == 2 -> 'mid_season_drainage' (single drainage); else 'upland_or_dry_seeded'. Step 2 (baseline EFc from IPCC 2019 Refinement Vol 4 Ch 5 Table 5.11, regional baseline emission factor for continuously flooded fields without organic amendments): EFc_kg_CH4_ha_day = { Asia.continental: 1.32, Asia.SE: 1.22, Asia.S: 0.85, Europe: 1.56, NorthAmerica: 0.65, Africa: 1.19, default_global: 1.19 } (regional pick from cell.country → IPCC region). Step 3 (water-regime scaling factor SFw from Table 5.12): SFw = { 'continuous_flood': 1.00, 'mid_season_drainage': 0.71, 'AWD': 0.52, 'upland_or_dry_seeded': 0.00 }. Step 4 (pre-season water regime SFp from Table 5.13, default 'non-flooded pre-season >180 d'): SFp = 0.68 unless explicit pre-season flooding flag, then SFp = 1.00 (short-flooded) or 1.90 (long-flooded >30 d). Step 5 (organic amendment SFo from Table 5.14, default no amendment): SFo = 1.00 unless straw incorporation flag is set, then SFo = 1 + (ROA · CFOA)^0.59 with CFOA = 0.19 for straw incorporated <30 d before cultivation, 0.29 for straw <30 d compost, 0.05 for FYM, 0.27 for green manure. Step 6 (Q10 temperature correction from Yan et al. 2005, IPCC-cited): T_mod = exp(0.0917 · (T_paddy_°C − 25)). Step 7 (final): CH4_kg_ha_season = EFc · SFw · SFp · SFo · cultivation_period_days · T_mod. Optional cumulative-NDVI biomass refinement [+/− 25 %] is RESEARCH-TIER ONLY and disabled at the IPCC Tier-2 default.

output.explanation: Modelled seasonal CH₄ emission per hectare following IPCC 2019 Refinement Vol 4 Ch 5 Equation 5.1, EFi = EFc · SFw · SFp · SFo. Convert to CO₂e by × 27.9 (IPCC AR6 100-yr GWP for non-fossil CH₄). The AWD vs continuous-flood DELTA (continuous_flood EFi minus AWD EFi) × project area is what feeds Verra VM0042 + AWD methodology module CV2 and Gold Standard MMM-PRA credit volume. NOT a measurement — explicitly labelled `model:'IPCC_2019_Tier2'` in the receipt; ground-truth on ≥1 eddy-covariance flux tower is REQUIRED before claiming credit issuance under VM0042.

algorithms.when_to_use: Rice-paddy carbon-credit MRV (Boomitra, Nurture.farm, Varaha, Mooved); AWD adoption verification; national rice-methane inventory backfill where direct flux measurement is absent. Do NOT use on dry-seeded rice or upland rice — the IPCC Tier-2 scaling factors have no skill there (SFw = 0 by definition); use Tier-1 default or a dedicated dry-seeded model instead.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {bands:['indices.ndwi','sentinel1_raw','indices.ndvi'], from:sowing, to:harvest}; POST /v1/recall {bands:['weather.temperature_2m']}; classify regime; apply IPCC 2019 Equation 5.1 with regional EFc lookup
algorithms.citation: IPCC. 2019. 2019 Refinement to the 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, Volume 4 (AFOLU) Chapter 5 'Cropland', Section 5.5 'Rice Cultivations'. Equation 5.1 (EFi = EFc · SFw · SFp · SFo); Table 5.11 (regional baseline EFc for continuously flooded fields, default global value 1.19 kg CH4/ha/day with regional overrides Asia.continental 1.32, Asia.SE 1.22, Asia.S 0.85, Europe 1.56, NorthAmerica 0.65, Africa 1.19); Table 5.12 (water-regime scaling factor SFw during cultivation period: continuously flooded 1.00, intermittently flooded single aeration 0.71, intermittently flooded multiple aerations / AWD 0.52, regular rainfed 0.54, drought-prone 0.16, deep water 0.06); Table 5.13 (pre-season water-regime SFp: non-flooded pre-season <180 d default 1.22, non-flooded pre-season >180 d 0.68, flooded pre-season >30 d 1.90, flooded pre-season <30 d 1.00); Table 5.14 (organic amendment SFo via SFo = (1 + ΣROA · CFOA)^0.59 with CFOA = 1.00 straw <30 d, 0.19 straw >30 d, 0.29 compost, 0.05 FYM, 0.27 green manure). URL https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch05_Cropland.pdf. Yan, X. et al. 2005. Statistical analysis of the major variables controlling methane emission from rice fields. Global Change Biology 11(7):1131–1141. DOI 10.1111/j.1365-2486.2005.00976.x (Q10 = 2.5 temperature scaling). Bouvet, A. et al. 2018. Sentinel-1 SAR backscatter under rice canopy. RSE 215:228–243 (γ⁰ VV < −17 dB standing-water signature). Verra Verified Carbon Standard. 2024. VM0042 v2.1 Improved Agricultural Land Management + AWD methodology module CV2.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: once per rice season (typically 110–150 d). Mid-season interim estimates can be issued every 10 days from sowing for monitoring dashboards but credit issuance MUST anchor on the full-season trajectory.
algorithms.accuracy_band: ±25–30 % at the seasonal-flux magnitude level (IPCC 2019 Vol 4 Ch 5 stated Tier-2 uncertainty for rice CH₄, dominated by the SFw uncertainty range of 0.46–0.59 for AWD in Table 5.12). The AWD-vs-continuous-flood DISCRIMINATION (which is what Verra VM0042 actually monetises) lifts to >90 % concordance with eddy-covariance ground-truth when at least one flux tower in the project region anchors the EFc — that's the operationally meaningful accuracy, not the absolute flux magnitude.
algorithms.pitfalls: 1. Regional EFc selection (Table 5.11) MUST use the cell's IPCC region (default global 1.19 kg CH4/ha/day is a fallback); silently using the global value in South Asia under-estimates by ≈29 % (S Asia EFc 0.85) and over-estimates in Europe by ≈31 % (Europe EFc 1.56). 2. The water-regime classifier from NDWI assumes Sentinel-2 cloud-free passes spanning the cultivation period; under monsoon cloud cover (>60 % of S2 passes lost) the regime call MUST fall back to Sentinel-1 γ⁰ VV — without S1 fallback, mid-season-drainage and AWD become indistinguishable. 3. SFp (pre-season) defaults to 0.68 (non-flooded >180 d) — if the cell was a fallow-flooded paddy or a fish-rice rotation, this under-estimates by up to 2.8×; flag with an explicit pre-season-flood input when available. 4. SFo (organic amendment) defaults to 1.00 (no amendment) — straw incorporation within 30 d of cultivation can lift CH4 by 2–4×; agricultural-survey or remote-detection of post-harvest straw burn/incorporation should override. 5. NOT a substitute for eddy-covariance flux towers under Verra VM0042 issuance — this algorithm produces the Tier-2 INVENTORY estimate; the credit-issuance methodology requires at least one project-specific flux measurement per agro-ecological zone.

inputs.explanation: Daily max-temperature series (NASA POWER `t2m_max` preferred when available; falls back to forecast.temperature_2m hourly max-of-day).
Daily min-temperature series (NASA POWER `t2m_min` preferred).

algorithms.formula: GDD_d = max(0, ((min(Tmax_d, T_upper) + max(Tmin_d, T_base)) / 2) - T_base) where T_base, T_upper are crop-specific (e.g. maize: 10/30 °C; wheat: 0/26 °C; rice: 10/35 °C); cumulative_GDD = Σ GDD_d from sowing date to date_t

output.explanation: Cumulative growing degree-days from sowing. Crop-specific phenology thresholds (e.g. maize tasseling ~ 750 GDD, wheat anthesis ~ 1100 GDD) drive the 'what stage is this plot at?' answer that EVERY downstream stress / yield / N-uptake algorithm depends on. McMaster-Wilhelm modified algorithm (clamp Tmin to T_base, Tmax to T_upper) prevents over-counting on cold nights and heat-stress days.

algorithms.when_to_use: Phenology-aware everything: stress-baseline windowing, irrigation scheduling, fertiliser-application timing, harvest-date forecasting. Surface alongside any crop_stress / n_uptake / yield call so the downstream agent knows whether the plot is at vegetative / reproductive / grain-fill stage.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/backfill {cell, band:'weather.temperature_2m', from:sowing, to:date_t} → daily Tmax/Tmin → apply McMaster-Wilhelm formula
algorithms.citation: McMaster, G.S. & Wilhelm, W.W. 1997. Growing degree-days: one equation, two interpretations. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology 87:291–300. Miller, P., Lanier, W. & Brandt, S. 2001. Using growing degree days to predict plant stages. MSU Extension MT200103AG. Snyder, R.L. 1985. Hand calculating degree days. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology 35:353–358.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: daily, anchored to user-supplied sowing date. Required as a phenology input by typed_stress_attribution@1, n_uptake_ndre@1, and crop_yield_proxy@1.
algorithms.accuracy_band: ±50 GDD per ±1 °C systematic bias in the temperature inputs. NASA POWER vs in-situ stations: RMSE ~1.2 °C → ~50 GDD/season uncertainty over a 1500 GDD season ≈ 3% — well inside crop-stage-detection tolerance.

inputs.explanation: Annual precipitation series for the R-factor (rainfall erosivity). NASA POWER 1981→ daily; aggregate to MFI (modified Fournier index) over ≥10 years for a stable R.
Clay fraction → K-factor (soil erodibility) per Williams 1995 EPIC equation. Higher clay = lower K (more aggregate stability).
Sand fraction → K-factor. Combined with silt (= 1000 − clay − sand) and SOC drives erodibility.
Soil Organic Carbon → K-factor; higher SOC suppresses erodibility (organic matter binds aggregates).
Elevation surface used to derive slope length λ (m) and slope angle θ — feeds the LS-factor (slope-length × slope-steepness, McCool et al. 1987).
Land-cover class drives the C-factor (cover-management) lookup: cropland 0.20–0.40, grassland 0.05, shrubs 0.03, forest 0.003, bare 1.0 (Panagos et al. 2015 reference values).

Sentinel-2 Bare Soil Index — current bare-soil fraction modulates the C-factor at 10 m. C-factor base value from ESA WorldCover gets multiplied by clamp(BSI/0.20, 0.5, 1.5) so a fallow crop pixel inherits a higher C than a vigorously-canopied one within the same WorldCover class (Borrelli 2017 dynamic-C approach).

Sentinel-1 γ⁰ VV — recent backscatter informs surface-roughness contribution to the LS-factor. A sharp γ⁰ rise (tilled / freshly-bare) raises LS by ~10%; γ⁰ stable (residue-covered or canopy) keeps LS at the DEM baseline. All-weather variance source for monsoon belts where S2 BSI is cloud-blocked.

algorithms.formula: R = MFI_to_R(MFI, regional_eq); MFI = Σ_month (P_month² / P_year);  K = 0.0034 + 0.0405·exp(-0.5·((log10(D_g)+1.659)/0.7101)²)  where D_g is geometric mean particle size from sand/silt/clay (Wischmeier 1978 nomograph); LS_base = (λ/22.13)^m · (65.41·sin²θ + 4.56·sinθ + 0.065) where m=0.5 if slope_pct≥5 (McCool 1987); LS = LS_base · clamp(1 + 0.10*z_sentinel1, 0.9, 1.15);  C_base = lookup(esa_worldcover.lc_2021);  C = C_base · clamp(indices.bsi/0.20, 0.5, 1.5)  (Borrelli 2017 dynamic-C);  P = 1.0 (no support practice, conservative); A = R · K · LS · C · P

output.explanation: Annual soil loss (USLE/RUSLE), tonnes per hectare per year. Threshold ladder per UN-FAO and EU JRC: ≤2 = tolerable, 2–5 = low, 5–10 = moderate, 10–20 = high, >20 = severe (NABARD subsidy threshold 10 t/ha/yr in India). Receipt MUST list each factor (R, K, LS, C, P) so an auditor can challenge any single component without re-running the chain. ALWAYS surface the dominant factor — most erosion answers reduce to either LS (steep slopes) or C (bare crops in monsoon).

algorithms.when_to_use: Watershed / plot erosion screening, NABARD soil-conservation subsidy verification, EU CAP eco-scheme conditionality, carbon-project additionality (eroded plots can't bank SOC). Pair with `soc_change_credit@1` — accelerated erosion debits the carbon ledger and SHOULD be deducted from issuable credits.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/backfill {cell, band:'power.precip', from: t-10yr, to: t}; POST /v1/recall {bands:['soilgrids.{clay,sand,soc}_0_30cm','copdem30m.elevation_mean','esa_worldcover.lc_2021']}; apply formula
algorithms.citation: Wischmeier, W.H. & Smith, D.D. 1978. Predicting Rainfall Erosion Losses — A Guide to Conservation Planning. USDA Agriculture Handbook 537. Renard, K.G. et al. 1997. Predicting Soil Erosion by Water: A Guide to Conservation Planning with the Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE). USDA Agriculture Handbook 703. Panagos, P. et al. 2015. The new assessment of soil loss by water erosion in Europe. Environmental Science & Policy 54:438–447. McCool, D.K. et al. 1987. Revised slope steepness factor for the Universal Soil Loss Equation. Trans. ASAE 30:1387–1396.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: annual (R-factor recomputed over a 10-yr rolling rainfall window per WMO guidance; K-factor static; LS-factor static at the 30 m DEM resolution; C-factor refresh on every Sentinel-2 cloud-free pass via dynamic-BSI modulation per Borrelli 2017).
algorithms.accuracy_band: RUSLE absolute uncertainty ±50% at plot scale (vs. erosion-pin or sediment-trap measurements; Renard 1997 §1.5). Skill at ranking plots and regions is materially better — use for relative prioritisation and threshold compliance, not absolute t/ha quotation. Dynamic C-factor from S2 BSI + S1 γ⁰ improves within-season bare-vs-canopy discrimination by ~25% over a static WorldCover-only C (Panagos 2015 vs. Borrelli 2017 European cross-validation).

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 red (665 nm); high reflectance over salt crust.
SWIR1 (1610 nm); strong absorption depression over salinized bare soil. SI-T (Khan 2005) anchors here.
SWIR2 (2190 nm); secondary salinity-absorption window.
Salinity is detectable only on bare or sparsely-vegetated soil (NDVI < 0.3); the algorithm returns Absence over canopy.
SOC informs the SOC-vs-salt confusion case — salt crust and dry low-SOC soils share a SWIR signature; SOC < 5 g/kg + B11/B12 ratio confirms salinity, > 15 g/kg ambiguous.

algorithms.formula: if BSI < 0.05 -> Absence{reason:'canopy_dominated'}; SI_T = 100 * sqrt(B04 * B11);  ratio_SWIR = B11 / B12;  salinity_class = if SI_T > 18.0 AND ratio_SWIR > 1.05 AND soc_0_30cm < 8.0 -> 'severe'; else if SI_T > 12.0 AND ratio_SWIR > 1.02 -> 'moderate'; else if SI_T > 8.0 -> 'mild'; else 'none'

output.explanation: Plot-level soil salinity hotspot screening. Khan 2005 SI-T threshold (12-18) plus SWIR ratio (Allbed 2014) discriminate genuine salt crust from organic dark soil. NOT a calibrated EC measurement — receipts MUST cite this as 'screening grade' not 'lab grade'. Pair with EC1:5 lab samples on flagged hotspots before any reclamation prescription.

algorithms.when_to_use: State-government coastal-reclamation programs (Gujarat saline tracts, Sundarbans), CGWB groundwater-irrigation salinity surveys, insurance fraud-screening on yield-loss claims that should correlate with salinity. Do NOT use over canopy or wet soil (water in pores erases SWIR signal).
algorithms.citation: Khan, N.M. et al. 2005. Assessment of hydrosaline land degradation by using a simple approach of remote sensing indicators. Agric. Water Manag. 77:96–109. Allbed, A. & Kumar, L. 2013. Soil salinity mapping and monitoring in arid and semi-arid regions using remote sensing technology. Adv. Remote Sens. 2:373–385. Allbed, A. et al. 2014. Mapping and modelling spatial variation in soil salinity. Geoderma 230-231:1–8.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: every Sentinel-2 cloud-free pass during dry season (post-harvest bare-soil window). Outside dry season SI-T noise dominates; rely on the existing post-harvest scan.
algorithms.accuracy_band: 75–85% precision discriminating salinity-class >= 'moderate' vs. 'none' against EC1:5 ground samples in arid/semi-arid plots (Allbed 2014). Substantially worse in subhumid/humid where SOC variability confounds the SWIR signal.

inputs.explanation: Per-Sentinel-2-revisit NDVI series across the agent-supplied search window (typically pre-monsoon to early post-monsoon for kharif, or late autumn for rabi). Sowing defined as the first NDVI inflection rising above NDVI_baseline + 0.05 sustained for ≥3 observations (Sakamoto 2005; Bolton-Friedl 2013).
Sentinel-1 γ⁰ VV drop ≥1.5 dB straddling the candidate sowing date = tillage / land preparation signature (Vreugdenhil 2018). Independent confirmation under cloud.
GDD accumulation from candidate sowing date — emergence requires ≥50–100 GDD for major crops; if not reached within 14 d, candidate is rejected.

algorithms.formula: candidates = { t : NDVI(t) - NDVI(t-revisit) > 0.05 AND NDVI(t) > 0.20 AND NDVI(t+revisit) > NDVI(t) }; for each candidate, require sentinel1_raw_drop(t-7d, t+7d) >= 1.5 dB OR persistence: NDVI(t+30d) - NDVI(t) > 0.15;  sowing_date = argmin(candidate); if no candidate in [search_start, search_end] -> Absence

output.explanation: Unix epoch second of the detected sowing date. RMSE ~9 days vs. agronomist-recorded sowing per Bolton & Friedl 2013 validation across 18 US Midwest counties; Indian smallholder validation by Singh et al. 2024 reports 7–12 d RMSE depending on cloud cover. ALWAYS surface a `confidence_window` field (typically ±7 d) so downstream phenology and yield models can propagate the uncertainty.

algorithms.when_to_use: Insurance sowing-window verification (PMFBY mandates documented sowing for premium/claim eligibility), warehouse capacity planning at Arya FPOs (predict harvest = sowing + days-to-maturity from `gdd_phenology@1`), agri-input timing recommendations.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {bands:['indices.ndvi','sentinel1_raw'], from:search_start, to:search_end}; POST /v1/backfill {band:'power.t2m', from:candidate, to:candidate+14d}; apply formula
algorithms.citation: Sakamoto, T. et al. 2005. A crop phenology detection method using time-series MODIS data. Remote Sensing of Environment 96:366–374. Bolton, D.K. & Friedl, M.A. 2013. Forecasting crop yield using remotely sensed vegetation indices and crop phenology metrics. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology 173:74–84. Vreugdenhil, M. et al. 2018. Sentinel-1 cross-ratio and vegetation optical depth: a comparison over Europe. Remote Sensing 10:1396.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: once per cropping season per plot, anchored to the user-supplied search window. Re-run only if the upstream Sentinel-2 archive backfills new cloud-free observations into the search window.
algorithms.accuracy_band: RMSE ~9 d on plots ≥ 0.5 ha with ≤ 30% monsoon cloud cover. Degrades to RMSE 14+ d in heavy-monsoon zones — surface SAR-only fallback explicitly when optical observations < 3 in the search window.

inputs.explanation: Senescence: sharp NDVI drop > 0.20 over 14 d signals harvest, NOT senescence-only — decoupled from gradual maturation.
NDTI rise post-harvest = exposed crop residue (cellulose absorption shifts vs. healthy canopy). Bowman et al. 2024.
B11 reflectance jump = bare-soil/residue signature, robust to NDVI noise.
Sentinel-1 γ⁰ VV rise post-harvest (lodging / stubble structural change) — the cloud-permitting confirmation channel for monsoon-overlapping rabi harvest in north-Indian wheat.

algorithms.formula: candidate_t = first t in window where NDVI(t-revisit) - NDVI(t) > 0.20 AND NDVI(t) < 0.30;  confirm_residue = NDTI(t+revisit) - NDTI(t-revisit) > 0.05; confirm_dryness = B11(t+revisit) - B11(t-revisit) > 0.05; confirm_sar = sentinel1_raw(t+revisit) - sentinel1_raw(t-revisit) > 1.0 dB; harvest_date = candidate_t IF (confirm_residue OR confirm_dryness OR confirm_sar); else Absence

output.explanation: Unix epoch second of detected harvest date. RMSE 5–10 d for major cereals (Whitcraft 2015; Mandanici 2022). Pair with `sowing_date_detection@1` and `gdd_phenology@1` to derive growing-season length and validate against crop-specific phenology priors. Triple-confirmation (NDVI drop + NDTI rise + B11 jump) lifts precision >90% but reduces recall ~80% in heavy cloud — surface BOTH precision and recall in the receipt.

algorithms.when_to_use: Storage capacity planning at FPOs (predict warehouse inflow ±5–10 d), trader procurement scheduling, mandi-arrival forecasting, post-harvest residue-burning enforcement (couples to `residue_burn_multisensor@1`).
algorithms.citation: Whitcraft, A.K. et al. 2015. A framework for defining spatially explicit Earth observation requirements for a global agricultural monitoring initiative (GEOGLAM). Remote Sensing 7:1461–1481. Mandanici, E. et al. 2022. Sentinel-2-based crop calendar metrics for European agriculture. RSE 270:112873. Bowman, T. et al. 2024. NDTI for residue-cover monitoring at field scale. Computers & Electronics in Agriculture 217:108652.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: once per cropping season; retro-detect only — no real-time forecast. Storage planners should pair with `gdd_phenology@1` for forecast.
algorithms.accuracy_band: RMSE 5–10 d on cereals with ≥3 cloud-free observations across the senescence window. Tropical/wet harvest seasons degrade detectability — surface as low-confidence when monsoon cloud > 60%. Adding the Sentinel-1 confirmation channel keeps recall >80% even at 90% monsoon cloud (Veloso 2017).

inputs.explanation: Pre-event vs post-event NDVI window; the loss percentage is a calibrated function of |ΔNDVI/NDVI_pre|.
If the event is fire/heat, NBR drop adds severity dimension separate from greenness.
If the event is flood, NDWI persistence ≥ 5 d straddling event date confirms inundation.
γ⁰ VV drop (water under canopy) or rise (lodging) is the cloud-permitting evidence path during monsoon.
ERA5 precip anomaly over the event window confirms the meteorological proximate cause; required for PMFBY-grade loss certification.

algorithms.formula: ndvi_pre  = max(NDVI over [event_start - 21d, event_start]);  ndvi_post = min(NDVI over [event_end, event_end + 21d]); loss_frac_optical = clamp01((ndvi_pre - ndvi_post) / ndvi_pre); loss_frac_sar    = clamp01((sigma0_pre - sigma0_post) / 5.0);  loss_class = if max(loss_frac_optical, loss_frac_sar) >= 0.50 -> 'total'; >= 0.33 -> 'severe'; >= 0.15 -> 'moderate'; >= 0.05 -> 'mild'; else 'none'; require era5.precip event-window anomaly OR explicit event_kind for PMFBY-grade evidence

output.explanation: PMFBY-grade plot loss verdict, signed with both optical and SAR fact_cids and the ERA5 precip-anomaly fact_cid. Class boundaries match PMFBY 2024-25 yield-loss bracket thresholds (5/15/33/50%) so the receipt drops directly into AIC's claim-payout schedule. Surface `loss_frac_optical`, `loss_frac_sar`, and `disagreement_flag` if they diverge by > 0.20 — that's the auditor's hook.

algorithms.when_to_use: PMFBY claim assessment (₹15K Cr+ premium pool; satellite verification mandated by IRDAI Order F.No.IRDAI/AGR/REG/CIR/130/12/2017), parametric-payout validation, ESG-grade insurance subrogation. Always pair with `parametric_trigger@1` for the event-window precondition.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {bands:['indices.ndvi','indices.nbr','indices.ndwi','sentinel1_raw','era5.precip'], from:event_start - 21d, to:event_end + 21d}; apply formula → emit signed classification
algorithms.citation: PMFBY 2.0 Operational Guidelines 2020 (Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers' Welfare, Govt of India). Tucker, C.J. et al. 2005. An extended AVHRR 8-km NDVI dataset compatible with MODIS and SPOT vegetation NDVI data. International Journal of Remote Sensing 26:4485–4498. Tellman, B. et al. 2021. Satellite imaging reveals increased proportion of population exposed to floods. Nature 596:80–86. Boschetti, L. et al. 2015. MODIS Collection 6 active fire and burned-area products: validation and comparison. RSE 159:158–170.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: event-driven — triggered by a parametric event (drought, flood, hail, fire) or a farmer's claim filing. Pre/post windows are 21 d each; do NOT shorten because cloud cover collapses confidence.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Optical-only loss-class accuracy 80–88% vs. AIC field-survey ground truth (Yagci 2015 corroborated on Indian PMFBY pilots). Adding SAR cross-validation lifts to 88–92% in cloud-prone seasons.

inputs.explanation: Per-year peak EVI (cloud-permitting Mar-Apr or Sep-Oct depending on hemisphere). Mann-Kendall on ≥6-yr series tests for monotonic decline.
Multi-year increase in BSI = expanding bare patches = degradation, even when a one-year EVI dip masks.
Hansen GFC loss year — cell-level forest loss is direct LDN evidence per UNCCD SDG 15.3.1 sub-indicator (a).
Slope > 8% amplifies degradation severity; surfaced in the explanation but not in the score (separation of structure).

algorithms.formula: tau, p_value = mann_kendall(evi_peak series, n>=6); s_evi = if tau<0 AND p<0.10 -> clamp01(-tau); else 0;  s_bsi = clamp01((bsi_recent_mean - bsi_baseline_mean) / 0.10); s_forest = step(hansen.loss_year >= 2001 within polygon); degradation_score = 0.50*s_evi + 0.30*s_bsi + 0.20*s_forest; class = if score >= 0.6 -> 'declining'; else if score >= 0.3 -> 'stable_at_risk'; else 'stable_or_improving'

output.explanation: UNCCD-aligned land-degradation neutrality (LDN, SDG 15.3.1) sub-indicator composite. The three sub-signals (productivity decline, bare-soil expansion, forest loss) are the three SDG 15.3.1 sub-indicators (a), (b), (c) UN-mandated for LDN reporting. Receipt MUST cite all three sub-indicators independently — UNCCD requires sub-indicator transparency, not just a composite.

algorithms.when_to_use: UNCCD SDG 15.3.1 national/sub-national reporting, NABARD watershed-restoration eligibility, ESG land-stewardship attestations, India's National Land Restoration Programme (NLRP). Use polygon (`/v1/recall_polygon`) — single-cell LDN is meaningless; the indicator is a population statistic.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/trajectory {band:'indices.evi', from: t-10yr, to: t}; POST /v1/recall {bands:['indices.bsi','hansen.loss_year','copdem30m.elevation_mean']}; apply Mann-Kendall + composite
algorithms.citation: UNCCD 2017. Methodological note for SDG indicator 15.3.1: Proportion of land that is degraded over total land area. Sims, N.C. et al. 2021. Good Practice Guidance for SDG Indicator 15.3.1 (V2.0). UNCCD Global Mechanism. Mann, H.B. 1945. Nonparametric tests against trend. Econometrica 13:245–259. Kendall, M.G. 1975. Rank Correlation Methods (4th ed.). Charles Griffin.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: annual; Mann-Kendall requires ≥6 years for statistical power, ≥10 years for reliable LDN reporting. Re-run with each new full year of Sentinel-2 archive.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Mann-Kendall trend p<0.10 detection power ~85% on 6-yr series; >95% on 10-yr series (Yue & Wang 2002 simulation studies). Composite score is editorial; the three sub-indicators are the auditable units.

inputs.explanation: spi_meteorological_drought@1 with the contract-defined window (typically SPI-3).
water_consensus@1 instantaneous probability OR water_likelihood_from_vv@1 plus surface_water.recurrence baseline for an event-anomaly framing.
Cumulative ERA5 precip over the contract window for excess-rain triggers (cyclone tail, cloudburst).

algorithms.formula: trigger = trip(SPI <= contract.drought_threshold) OR trip(flood_prob >= contract.flood_threshold for >= contract.duration_days) OR trip(weather.temperature_2m >= contract.heat_threshold for >= contract.heat_consecutive_days) OR trip(era5_precip_window >= contract.rain_threshold);  payout_multiplier = max trigger of: linear interpolation between threshold and cap as defined in contract.tranches

output.explanation: Contract-grade parametric-trigger evaluation. 0 = no trigger; 1 = full trigger; intermediate = tranched payout proportion. Receipt cites every input fact_cid plus the contract's `algorithm_cid` snapshot, so a re-run on the published manifests CIDs reproduces the trigger evaluation byte-for-byte. This is the legal-evidence layer underneath any parametric agri product.

algorithms.when_to_use: Indian parametric agri insurance (growing 11.3% CAGR per IRDAI 2024), Swiss Re / Munich Re / RGA reinsurer-grade index products, FPO climate-event guarantees. The signed receipt — citing emem's responder pubkey + fact_cids + algorithm_cid + contract snapshot — replaces the per-event field-survey of indemnity products.
algorithms.primitive: Compose: spi_meteorological_drought@1 + water_consensus@1 + recall on weather.temperature_2m, era5.precip; then apply contract thresholds
algorithms.deterministic_note: The threshold ladder is contract-supplied — the algorithm itself is deterministic in fact_cids + contract; the contract MUST be content-addressed too.
algorithms.citation: IRDAI 2023. Master circular on parametric-index agri insurance products. Swiss Re Institute. 2023. Parametric insurance: a fast and transparent settlement option. Skees, J.R. & Collier, B. 2008. The potential of weather index insurance for spurring a green revolution in Africa. World Bank Working Paper. WMO/UNDP 2022. Index Insurance Guidance Note.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: daily during the contract risk window (typically 90–180 d for drought / cyclone covers); event-driven evaluation outside the window. Receipts are emitted on every state change, not on a poll cadence.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Trigger reproducibility = 100% (deterministic given inputs + contract). Basis-risk vs. on-ground indemnity loss is a separate question — quote Skees & Collier 2008 ~15% basis-risk for well-designed weather-index products.

inputs.explanation: Cumulative precipitation over the SPI window (default 90 d for agricultural drought; SPI-1 for monthly anomalies; SPI-12 for hydrological drought). NASA POWER 1981→ or CHIRPS 1981→ are the recommended sources because both have ≥30 yr of record needed for the gamma fit.

algorithms.formula: let X = Σ precipitation_mm over window of length n_days; fit Γ(α,β) parameters to the cell's same-window same-DOY historical samples (≥30 yr required); transform_to_normal: SPI = Φ⁻¹(Γ_cdf(X | α,β)); interpretation = if SPI <= -2.0 -> 'extreme_drought'; <= -1.5 -> 'severe_drought'; <= -1.0 -> 'moderate_drought'; <= -0.5 -> 'mild_drought'; <= 0.5 -> 'near_normal'; <= 1.0 -> 'mildly_wet'; <= 1.5 -> 'moderately_wet'; <= 2.0 -> 'very_wet'; else 'extremely_wet'

output.explanation: Standardized Precipitation Index. Z-score of gamma-fitted accumulated precipitation. Agricultural drought monitoring uses SPI-3 (90 d) for cropping-season screening; SPI-12 for hydrological/reservoir context. McKee 1993 thresholds are the WMO-endorsed reference; surface BOTH the numeric SPI and the categorical class so an agent can either chart it or trigger a workflow. Window MUST be declared in the receipt — SPI-1 and SPI-12 on the same date can disagree by a category.

algorithms.when_to_use: Agricultural-drought monitoring, parametric-payout insurance triggers (PMFBY, ARC), reservoir-management context. Pair with `precip_intensity_class@1` for short-duration severity AND with NDMI-anomaly for crop response. SPI is precipitation-only — for full agricultural drought use SPEI (precipitation − PET) when ET is wired.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/backfill {cell, band:'weather.precipitation_mm', from:date - 30yr, to:date} → window-sum → fit Gamma → SPI
algorithms.citation: McKee, T.B., Doesken, N.J. & Kleist, J. 1993. The relationship of drought frequency and duration to time scales. 8th Conf. on Applied Climatology, AMS, 179–184. WMO. 2012. Standardized Precipitation Index User Guide. WMO-No.1090. Guttman, N.B. 1999. Accepting the SPI: a calculation algorithm. JAWRA 35(2):311–322. Stagge, J.H. et al. 2015. Candidate distributions for climatological drought indices (SPI and SPEI). Int. J. Climatol. 35:4027–4040.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: monthly for SPI-1 / SPI-3; quarterly for SPI-6 / SPI-12. Re-fit the gamma parameters every 5 yr to incorporate climate-change non-stationarity (use a moving 30-yr reference window per WMO guidance).
algorithms.accuracy_band: Gamma-fit standard error < 0.1 SPI units when ≥30 yr of complete record is available. With NASA POWER (1981→) every cell on Earth has ≥40 yr by 2026 = sound footing. Reduce confidence below SPI ±2 if record-length < 30 yr (e.g. local stations).

temporal_recipe.label: drought_compounding
temporal_recipe.note: SPI itself reads a precipitation aggregate vs the 30-yr gamma fit; the recipe here adds NDVI and NDMI lookbacks so a downstream agent can see the crop-side response (vegetation + moisture) alongside the meteorological signal.

inputs.explanation: PM2.5 24-hour-average proxy. CAMS hourly forecasts approximate the 24-hour average that the US EPA AQI breakpoint table is defined against — adequate for a 'roughly Good / Moderate / Unhealthy' classification, not for regulatory reporting.

algorithms.formula: let p = cams.pm25; if p > 250.4 -> 6 (Hazardous); else if p > 150.4 -> 5 (Very Unhealthy); else if p > 55.4 -> 4 (Unhealthy); else if p > 35.4 -> 3 (Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups); else if p > 12.0 -> 2 (Moderate); else -> 1 (Good)

output.explanation: Integer 1–6 indexing the US EPA AQI category names in `output.values` (1-based). The integer is what the AST evaluates to so an agent can quote 'aqi_class@1 = 4' and look up 'Unhealthy' in the same registry record. Defined on PM2.5 alone — for full multi-pollutant AQI take the max of `aqi_class@1`, `aqi_pm10@1`, `aqi_no2@1`, etc. (registry will grow as those subindices land).

algorithms.when_to_use: Use when an agent has already pulled `cams.pm25` and wants a stable, cite-able AQI category instead of inventing thresholds. Pair with the pollutant value itself in the answer (`PM2.5 = 142 µg/m³, AQI category 4 / Unhealthy`).
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['cams.pm25']} → evaluate `aqi_class@1` Expr → integer 1–6 → look up label in output.values[index-1]
algorithms.citation: US EPA. Technical Assistance Document for the Reporting of Daily Air Quality — the Air Quality Index (AQI). EPA 454/B-18-007, Sep 2018, Table 2 (PM2.5 breakpoints, 24-hour averaging period).

inputs.explanation: Air temperature at 2 m.
Total cloud cover, 0–100.
Precipitation accumulated over the current tslot.
Wind speed at 10 m above ground.

algorithms.formula: let sky = if cloud_cover < 25 then 'clear' else if cloud_cover < 75 then 'partly cloudy' else 'overcast'; let precip = if precipitation_mm > 7.5 then ' with heavy rain' else if precipitation_mm > 2.5 then ' with rain' else if precipitation_mm > 0.1 then ' with light rain' else ''; let wind = if wind_speed_10m > 17 then ', windy' else ''; concat(sky, precip, ', ', round(temperature_2m), '°C', wind)

output.explanation: Human-readable one-liner. The `values[]` list illustrates representative outputs; the actual return string concatenates `<sky>` + optional `<rain>` + temperature suffix + optional `, windy`. Deterministic given the four inputs. Currently formula-only (no Expr AST) because the AST evaluator is f64→f64 today; a future `String`-typed Expr op would let `algorithm_outcomes[]` fire it.

algorithms.when_to_use: Use when an agent wants a short natural-language weather sentence to echo to a user, instead of dumping four numeric scalars. Pair with the underlying numbers in `inputs[]` so the user can drill in.
algorithms.citation: Editorial. The cloud-cover thresholds follow the Met Office plain-language convention (`<25% clear, 25–75% partly cloudy, >75% overcast`); the precipitation classes follow the WMO convention used in METAR (`light <2.5 mm/h, moderate 2.5–7.5, heavy >7.5`); the wind threshold (17 m/s ≈ Beaufort 8 'fresh gale') is when wind starts to become operationally noteworthy.

inputs.explanation: Land Surface Temperature (Kelvin) at the cell of interest — the centre of the 3×3 explicit-FD stencil.

Same band recalled at each of the 8 cell64 neighbours surrounding the centre. Per-neighbour fact CIDs are surfaced in `neighborhood_signed_at` so a verifier can re-fetch any one of them. Boundary cells are held at their initial values (Dirichlet BC) for the duration of the rollout.

algorithms.formula: u[i,j]^{n+1} = u[i,j]^n + α·Δt·(u[i+1,j] + u[i-1,j] + u[i,j+1] + u[i,j-1] − 4·u[i,j]) / Δx² ; subject to CFL: α·Δt/Δx² ≤ 0.25 (we run at 0.20 of the bound for round-off margin)

output.explanation: Forecast Land Surface Temperature at the centre cell after the requested horizon, in Kelvin. The response also surfaces `delta_k` (final − initial) and the full `neighborhood_initial_k` so an agent can plot the local stencil.

algorithms.when_to_use: Use when the user wants a short-horizon LST forecast (urban heat island propagation, surface-temperature evolution under heatwave conditions) at a specific cell. Default α=1e-6 m²/s matches urban surfaces (Oke 2017 §2.3 Table 2.4). Capped at 168 h (one week) — past that the 8-day MODIS composite stops being a representative initial condition.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/heat_solve {cell, hours_ahead, diffusivity_m2_per_s} → 9-cell stencil → explicit FTCS rollout → signed forecast
algorithms._deterministic_note: Fully deterministic given the 9 input fact CIDs + the request body. There is intentionally NO `evaluation` AST entry — the AST evaluator is f64→f64 and the heat solver is a many-step PDE rollout with neighbour state. The dispatcher is the REST handler at /v1/heat_solve, which signs the forecast with the responder identity and cites all 9 input fact CIDs in the receipt.
algorithms.citation: Crank, J. & Nicolson, P. 1947. A practical method for numerical evaluation of solutions of partial differential equations of the heat-conduction type. Proc. Camb. Philos. Soc. 43:50–67. Oke, T.R. 2017. Urban Climates §2.3 (urban surface diffusivity ≈1×10⁻⁶ m²/s). Courant, Friedrichs & Lewy 1928 (the 0.25 CFL bound for 2-D explicit FTCS).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: On demand — the 8-day MODIS composite refreshes every 8 days, so a re-call after the next composite drops gives an updated initial condition.
