algorithms.accuracy_band: First-order in time, second-order in space. Forecast skill drops with horizon; sub-hour horizons are well-resolved, multi-day horizons depend on whether the surface diffusivity assumption (urban vs vegetation vs water) matches the local cover.

inputs.explanation: Topo-bathymetric elevation along the seaward profile from `coastal_cell`. We negate to get positive depth (m below sea level) and compute the local phase speed c = √(g·h) at each cell. GMRT covers any global cell — including the open ocean — at a single signed value, which is what makes this primitive globally addressable.

algorithms.formula: u[i]^{n+1} = 2·u[i]^n − u[i]^{n-1} + (c[i]·Δt/Δx)²·(u[i+1]^n − 2·u[i]^n + u[i-1]^n) ; offshore BC = H_s·sin(2π·t/T) ; coastal BC = 0 (hard wall, arrival height read from u[N-2]) ; subject to CFL: c·Δt/Δx ≤ 1 (we run at 0.5)

output.explanation: Maximum wave amplitude reached at the coast over the integration window. Surfaces the full `depth_profile_m` and `phase_speed_profile_m_per_s` arrays so a verifier can re-derive c² = g·h at every step.

algorithms.when_to_use: Use for short-fetch swell-arrival prediction (storm-surge front, tsunami refraction warm-up, surf forecasting). The solver walks N cells seaward from the coastal cell (default 8 → 80 m profile at the active 10 m grid) along the GMRT bathymetric gradient. Returns 422 with a clear message if the coast is land-locked or the profile crosses onto land within 3 cells.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/wave_solve {coastal_cell, offshore_height_m, period_s, n_offshore_cells} → seaward bathymetric walk → explicit CTCS wave rollout → signed arrival
algorithms._deterministic_note: Fully deterministic given the depth-profile fact CIDs + the request body. There is intentionally NO `evaluation` AST entry — the AST evaluator is f64→f64 and the wave solver is a many-step PDE rollout with previous-step state. The dispatcher is the REST handler at /v1/wave_solve, which signs the arrival with the responder identity and cites every depth fact CID along the profile in the receipt.
algorithms.citation: Lighthill, J. 1978. Waves in Fluids §3.1 (linear shallow-water wave). Holthuijsen, L.H. 2007. Waves in Oceanic and Coastal Waters §5.3 (refraction-free 1-D propagation). Courant, Friedrichs & Lewy 1928 (the c·Δt/Δx ≤ 1 bound for explicit CTCS).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: On demand — GMRT depths are static, so the only inputs that change between calls are H_s and T from the user's offshore forcing. A typical operational use re-runs the solver each time a new offshore buoy report drops.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Linear shallow-water approximation — reliable for h ≫ H_s (deep-water limit) and breaks down in the surf zone where wave breaking + steepening dominate. The 1-D refraction-free assumption ignores cross-shore refraction; for that, the @2 version will sample a 2-D bathymetric patch.

inputs.explanation: Past `lookback_months` months of NDVI at the target cell. `indices.ndvi` is Tempo::Medium (~30-day slot), so each fact corresponds to one calendar month. The predictor uses the literal monthly values; if a 12-month lag is available in the lookback it dominates the seasonal carryover term, otherwise α's contribution falls back to the lookback mean (degraded gracefully).

algorithms.formula: y_{t+1} = α·(lag-12 NDVI or recent_mean if unavailable) + β·(last + slope) + γ·recent_mean, clamped to [-1, 1] ; α=0.6 (year-over-year carryover), β=0.3 (recent slope), γ=0.1 (long-term mean reversion). Coefficients are CLOSED-FORM, not learned.

output.explanation: Predicted NDVI for the next monthly tslot. Clamped to NDVI's physical range. Surfaces `predictor_coefficients`, `lag_12_used`, and `lag_12_fallback_to_recent_mean` so an agent can audit which terms drove the prediction.

algorithms.when_to_use: Use for one-month-ahead NDVI forecasting at a single agricultural / vegetation cell. Designed for crop-stress monitoring, growing-season tracking, and vegetation-anomaly anticipation. Returns 422 if no NDVI history exists at the cell — chain to `emem_backfill` first to seed history.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/jepa_predict {cell, band, lookback_months, forecast_horizon_months} → recall the lookback → AR(2) seasonal predictor → signed scalar
algorithms._deterministic_note: Fully deterministic given the input fact CIDs and the closed-form coefficients. The honest framing in the response says this is a CONSTRAINED JEPA-pattern predictor — closed-form AR(2), NOT a learned encoder/predictor MLP. v2 will train an actual encoder + predictor on the geotessera embedding pool.
algorithms.citation: Assran, M. et al. 2023. Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA). CVPR. Pettorelli, N. et al. 2005. Using the satellite-derived NDVI to assess ecological responses to environmental change. Trends Ecol. Evol. 20:503–510. Tucker, C.J. 1979. Red and photographic infrared linear combinations for monitoring vegetation. Remote Sens. Environ. 8:127–150. The α=0.6, β=0.3, γ=0.1 weights are editorial v1 — they reflect the seasonal-dominance, short-term-trend, and long-term-mean structure that the agricultural-NDVI literature consistently reports.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Monthly — there is one new NDVI tslot per calendar month in the active Tempo::Medium grid, so a re-call after the next monthly composite drops gives an updated forecast.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Closed-form AR(2) with seasonal lag has been the baseline for NDVI forecasting for two decades; reported skill scores in the literature land in the ρ=0.65–0.85 range over agricultural cropland with adequate lookback (≥18 months). Below 6 months of lookback the lag-12 term is unavailable and skill drops to ρ≈0.5 — surface this honestly via `lag_12_used`.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 NDVI at 10 m. Saturates above ~0.8 in dense canopies; the MSR transformation linearises the saturation tail.

algorithms.formula: let n = clamp(indices.ndvi, 0.05, 0.85); let SR = (1 + n) / (1 - n); let MSR = (SR - 1) / (sqrt(SR) + 1); LAI_m2_per_m2 = clamp(0.69 * MSR, 0, 8)

output.explanation: Single-sided green Leaf Area Index in m²/m². The Modified Simple Ratio reduces the asymptotic saturation of NDVI in closed canopies, giving a more linear response over LAI 0-6 typical of crops and broadleaf forests.

algorithms.when_to_use: Per-cell LAI for crop monitoring, canopy radiative-transfer inputs, evapotranspiration models, biomass studies. Prefer over raw NDVI when downstream physics needs an actual leaf-area metric.
algorithms.citation: Chen, J. 1996. Evaluation of vegetation indices and a modified simple ratio for boreal applications. Canadian Journal of Remote Sensing 22:229–242. The 0.69 LAI / MSR scalar is the boreal calibration; finer crop-specific calibration is left to the caller's downstream model.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting (Sentinel-2 revisit cadence at the equator).
algorithms.accuracy_band: ρ ≈ 0.7–0.85 vs in-situ destructive LAI in row crops with cloud-free imagery. MSR's saturation knee sits around LAI 6, above which the index loses sensitivity.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 NDVI at 10 m. Linearly mapped to fAPAR over the green vegetation range; clamped to [0,1] outside it.

algorithms.formula: fAPAR = clamp(1.24 * indices.ndvi - 0.168, 0, 1)

output.explanation: Fraction of incoming photosynthetically active radiation (400–700 nm) absorbed by the green canopy. Used directly in light-use-efficiency GPP models.

algorithms.when_to_use: Per-cell fAPAR for productivity, GPP/NPP estimation, ecosystem flux models. Cheaper drop-in for MODIS MOD15A2H at 10 m where the high-resolution sensor matters.
algorithms.citation: Myneni, R.B. & Williams, D.L. 1994. On the relationship between FAPAR and NDVI. Remote Sensing of Environment 49:200–211. The 1.24 / 0.168 coefficients are the Myneni piecewise-linear simplification widely used for global GPP products (Sims et al. 2008, Running et al. 2004).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Reported R² 0.6–0.8 against EOS-FAPAR validation sites; weakest in sparse canopies (LAI < 1) where the linear assumption underestimates absorption.

inputs.explanation: Normalized Difference Red-Edge index from Sentinel-2 B05 (705 nm) and B08 (842 nm) at 10 m. Sensitive to chlorophyll concentration where red-band-only NDVI saturates.

algorithms.formula: let r = clamp(indices.ndre, 0.0, 0.6); CCC_ug_per_cm2 = clamp(50.0 * r, 0, 80)

output.explanation: Canopy chlorophyll content in micrograms per square centimetre. Empirical linear inversion across the typical NDRE range; saturates around CCC 40–50 in dense canopies.

algorithms.when_to_use: Crop nitrogen status, fertiliser-rate maps, vineyard / orchard health when leaf-level chlorophyll is the question and NDVI has saturated.
algorithms.citation: Clevers, J.G.P.W. & Gitelson, A.A. 2013. Remote estimation of crop and grass chlorophyll and nitrogen content using red-edge bands on Sentinel-2 and -3. International Journal of Applied Earth Observation 23:344–351. Frampton, W.J. et al. 2013. Evaluating the capabilities of Sentinel-2 for quantitative estimation of biophysical variables in vegetation. ISPRS 82:83–92.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting.
algorithms.accuracy_band: R² 0.55–0.80 against destructive CCC samples in cropland; the linear simplification trades the upper-tail accuracy of full PROSAIL inversion for transparency and reproducibility.

inputs.explanation: Normalized Difference Moisture Index (B08 − B11) / (B08 + B11) at 10 m. Tracks leaf canopy moisture; falls under stress.
NDVI gates the score so non-vegetated pixels return 0 stress (only crops can be water-stressed).
Recent 2-m air temperature. CWSI rises with vapour-pressure deficit; T_air > 30 °C raises the proxy linearly.
Recent 2-m relative humidity. CWSI inversely scales with RH (drier air → higher transpiration demand).

algorithms.formula: let g = sigmoid(8 * (indices.ndvi - 0.25)); let m = clamp(indices.ndmi / 0.4, 0, 1); let dry_air_term = (1 - clamp(weather.relative_humidity / 100, 0, 1)); let heat_term = clamp((weather.temperature_2m - 30) / 10, 0, 1); CWSI = g * clamp(1 - m * (1 - 0.5 * heat_term - 0.3 * dry_air_term), 0, 1)

output.explanation: 0 = no stress (well-watered crop in mild weather), 1 = severe stress. Receipts surface `gating_factor`, `moisture_term`, `heat_term`, `dry_air_term` so an agronomist can audit which factor dominated.

algorithms.when_to_use: Field-scale irrigation triggers, drought-stress mapping, agronomy decision support. Pair with `gdd_phenology@1` to know whether the stress is at a sensitive growth stage.
algorithms.citation: Idso, S.B. et al. 1981. Normalizing the stress-degree-day parameter for environmental variability. Agricultural Meteorology 24:45–55. Hunt, E.R. & Rock, B.N. 1989. Detection of changes in leaf water content using near and middle-infrared reflectances. Remote Sensing of Environment 30:43–54.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; weather inputs refresh hourly.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Spectral CWSI proxies are correlated with thermal CWSI at R² 0.4–0.7 in row crops; the meteorological gating reduces false positives over cool-and-dry baselines.

temporal_recipe.label: field_water_stress_window
temporal_recipe.note: CWSI requires recent canopy moisture (S2) plus recent atmospheric demand (T_air + RH). Snapshot-only would miss the demand side.

inputs.explanation: Tessera annual 128-D foundation embedding at the cell. Compared by cosine similarity against an editorial library of crop archetype centroids.

algorithms.formula: let v = recall(geotessera @ cell); for archetype A in {rice_paddy, wheat, maize, soybean, oilpalm, sugarcane, vineyard, orchard, fallow, mixed_field}: score[A] = cos(v, centroid[A]); class = argmax(score); confidence = score[class] - score[second]

output.explanation: Most-similar crop archetype at the cell, plus a confidence margin. `mixed_field` returns when the top class margin is below 0.05; agents should treat that as 'no clear archetype'.

algorithms.when_to_use: Open-data crop typing in a cell when in-situ labels are absent. Cheap drop-in for downstream value-chain models (yield, water, fertiliser) that need a class, not a vector.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/find_similar {key:cell64, k:1, mode:'cosine'} against the published crop_archetype centroids
algorithms._deterministic_note: Result depends on the responder's loaded archetype centroid set. The centroids are content-addressed under `algorithms_cid`; two responders with the same algorithms_cid produce the same class. Centroid drift between manifest revisions is the only non-determinism.
algorithms.citation: Tessera v1 (annual foundation embedding for global EO, 2024). Editorial archetype centroids are derived from labelled reference samples in FAO WAPOR, MODIS Cropland Validation, and the Radiant Earth Spectrum suite.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual — Tessera vintages drop yearly; archetype matching is stable within a vintage.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Top-1 accuracy 0.65–0.80 on global out-of-sample validation cells when one of the 10 archetypes is the truth. Falls below 0.5 in mixed-cropping zones where the cell mixes two archetypes — surface as `mixed_field` confidence < 0.05.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 surface reflectance at 490 nm (10 m). Penetrates clear shallow water; attenuated faster than green, so the blue/green log ratio scales with depth.
Sentinel-2 surface reflectance at 560 nm (10 m). Used together with B02 in the Stumpf log-ratio bathymetry.

algorithms.formula: let eps = 0.001; let r_blue = max(s2.B02, eps); let r_green = max(s2.B03, eps); let ratio = ln(1000.0 * r_blue) / ln(1000.0 * r_green); depth_m = clamp(2300.0 * ratio - 0.5, 0, 25)

output.explanation: Estimated water depth in metres. Reliable in clear coastal water with sand-bottom contrast; degrades over turbid water, vegetation, dark substrates, and beyond ~25 m where blue extinction saturates.

algorithms.when_to_use: Coastal Satellite-Derived Bathymetry (SDB) for navigation charts in clear shallow water (Caribbean, Mediterranean, Red Sea, Pacific atolls). Pair with `water_turbidity_red_band@1` to gate cells where the assumption fails.
algorithms.citation: Stumpf, R.P., Holderied, K. & Sinclair, M. 2003. Determination of water depth with high-resolution satellite imagery over variable bottom types. Limnology and Oceanography 48:547–556.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; only useful at low tide / low-glint scenes.
algorithms.accuracy_band: RMSE 0.5–2 m vs lidar/multibeam in clear water 0–15 m. Errors grow above 15 m and over turbid or vegetated bottoms; the m1=2300, m0=0.5 coefficients are the global default and benefit from local tuning when ground truth exists.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 470–520 nm at 10 m. Attenuated by chlorophyll absorption around 440 nm — lower in chlorophyll-rich water.
Sentinel-2 540–580 nm at 10 m. Less affected by chlorophyll absorption — relatively higher in chlorophyll-rich water.

algorithms.formula: let eps = 0.001; let r = ln(max(s2.B03, eps) / max(s2.B02, eps)); chl_a_ug_per_L = clamp(pow(10.0, 0.4 - 3.3 * r + 1.5 * r * r), 0.01, 100)

output.explanation: Chlorophyll-a concentration in micrograms per litre. The OC-style polynomial captures the typical case-1 water relationship; case-2 (sediment-rich) water needs the NIR red-edge family of algorithms instead.

algorithms.when_to_use: Coastal and lake water-quality monitoring (algal bloom detection, eutrophication tracking, fisheries), under clear-sky case-1 conditions.
algorithms.citation: Gilerson, A.A. et al. 2010. Algorithms for remote estimation of chlorophyll-a in coastal and inland waters using red and near-infrared bands. Optics Express 18:24109–24125. The blue/green polynomial is adapted from NASA's OC4 family (O'Reilly et al. 1998) at Sentinel-2 wavelengths.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; algal blooms can shift inside that window so an early-warning workflow should re-call after every cloud-free pass.
algorithms.accuracy_band: R² 0.6–0.8 vs in-situ chl-a in case-1 water; over-estimates in turbid case-2 water — pair with `water_turbidity_red_band@1` and gate the chl estimate when turbidity > ~10 FNU.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 red band (650–680 nm) at 10 m. Suspended sediment scatters strongly in the red — so red surface reflectance is a robust turbidity proxy in inland and coastal water.

algorithms.formula: let r = clamp(s2.B04, 0.0, 0.49); turbidity_FNU = clamp(22.4 * r / (1 - 2 * r), 0, 1000)

output.explanation: Turbidity in Formazin Nephelometric Units (≈ NTU). Rises sharply when red reflectance saturates; clamp to 1000 to avoid the singularity at r=0.5.

algorithms.when_to_use: Inland-water turbidity tracking (rivers, reservoirs, estuaries, harbours), sediment-plume mapping after storms, dredging-impact monitoring.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['s2.B04']} → apply Nechad 2010 single-band relation in-process
algorithms.citation: Nechad, B., Ruddick, K.G. & Park, Y. 2010. Calibration and validation of a generic multisensor algorithm for mapping of total suspended matter in turbid waters. Remote Sensing of Environment 114:854–866. Doxaran, D. et al. 2002. A reflectance band ratio used to estimate suspended matter concentrations in sediment-dominated coastal waters. International Journal of Remote Sensing 23:5079–5085.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; flow events demand re-call after each clear pass.
algorithms.accuracy_band: R² 0.7–0.9 in turbid case-2 water; saturates above ~80 FNU where red reflectance plateaus and the 1/(1-2r) term explodes.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-1 GRD VV backscatter in decibels at 10 m. Open water is a near-specular reflector for C-band radar at low incidence: backscatter drops far below typical land values, so a single threshold separates water from land in most cloud-free and cloudy scenes alike.

algorithms.formula: let v = sentinel1_raw; class = if v < -16 then 'water' else 'land'

output.explanation: Two-class flood / open-water mask. The −16 dB threshold is the operationally common Otsu break for VV C-band over open land; pair with a roughness gate when wind-roughened water and dry sand both push toward the threshold.

algorithms.when_to_use: Rapid flood mapping after a storm, river overtopping, dam break, or coastal surge. SAR sees through clouds, so this primitive can answer 'what is flooded right now' when optical imagery is opaque.
algorithms.citation: Twele, A., Cao, W., Plank, S. & Martinis, S. 2016. Sentinel-1-based flood mapping: a fully automated processing chain. International Journal of Remote Sensing 37:2990–3004. Otsu, N. 1979. A threshold selection method from gray-level histograms. IEEE TSMC 9:62–66.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 6–12 day Sentinel-1 revisit, all-weather. Re-call within hours of a triggering event for emergency response.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy 0.85–0.95 for open-water flood pixels in flat terrain. Lower in mountainous shadow / layover, urban environments (corner reflectors), and densely vegetated wetlands; pair with the wetland classifier and a slope mask in those terrains.

inputs.explanation: 1024-D Tessera multi-year stack — eight annual 128-D vintages (2017–2024) concatenated. Year-over-year cosine change is computed from the relevant 128-D slices.

algorithms.formula: let v_t = slice(geotessera.multi_year, latest_year_offset, 128); let v_prev = slice(geotessera.multi_year, latest_year_offset - 128, 128); change = clamp(1 - cos(v_t, v_prev), 0, 2)

output.explanation: Cosine distance between this year's and last year's 128-D Tessera vector at the cell. 0 = identical, 1 = orthogonal, 2 = anti-correlated. Real-world changes — clearcut, harvest, urban expansion, flooding, wildfire — sit in the 0.05–0.4 range.

algorithms.when_to_use: Generic 'did this place change between consecutive years?' detector when you don't yet know what kind of change to look for. Cheap pre-filter before chaining to band-specific change scores.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['geotessera.multi_year']} → cosine over the latest two 128-D slices
algorithms.citation: Tessera v1 (annual foundation embedding for global EO, 2024). Cosine distance over learned EO embeddings is a widely used change-detection signal (Tian et al. 2023; Reed et al. 2023).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual — Tessera ships one 128-D vintage per calendar year.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Detects > 80 % of human-visible large-area change between consecutive vintages on validation grids; saturates for subtle within-class changes (e.g. crop rotation between similar archetypes).

inputs.explanation: Latest Sentinel-2 NDVI at the cell. The same-season slot a year earlier supplies the comparison baseline through the temporal_recipe.
Latest Sentinel-1 VV backscatter at the cell, compared against the same-season slot a year earlier.
Multi-year Tessera stack. The two most-recent 128-D slices supply the embedding-side change term.

algorithms.formula: let dn = abs(indices.ndvi - lookback(indices.ndvi, 365d)); let dv = abs(sentinel1_raw - lookback(sentinel1_raw, 365d)); let de = 1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), slice_prev(geotessera.multi_year)); let c_s2 = clamp(dn / 0.30, 0, 1); let c_s1 = clamp(dv / 4.0, 0, 1); let c_emb = clamp(de, 0, 1); score = sqrt((c_s2*c_s2 + c_s1*c_s1 + c_emb*c_emb) / 3)

output.explanation: Fused per-cell change index over one calendar year. The L2 mean across the three normalised channels is robust to a single noisy term — receipts surface c_s2, c_s1, c_emb so an analyst can see which sensor drove the alert.

algorithms.when_to_use: Confidence-weighted year-over-year change detection at 10 m when no single sensor is decisive. Use as the gate for a downstream typed classifier (deforestation, urbanisation, conversion).
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['indices.ndvi','sentinel1_raw','geotessera.multi_year']} with a 365-day temporal_recipe → apply L2-mean fusion
algorithms.citation: Editorial fusion of Twele 2016 (S1 dB change), Tucker 1979 (NDVI dynamics), Tessera 2024 (multi-year stack). The L2-mean normalisation pattern follows Healey et al. 2018 (LandTrendr ensemble).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; can be run quarterly with a 90-day lookback for sub-annual change.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Reported in editorial validation: ~85 % producer's accuracy on labelled deforestation polygons with ≥0.5 ha extent at 10 m. Reduces false positives 2–4× over any single-sensor baseline at the same threshold.

temporal_recipe.label: year_on_year_change
temporal_recipe.note: Each input is compared against its same-season counterpart 365 days earlier.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 SWIR1 (1610 nm) at 20 m, resampled to the active 10 m cell. Crystalline-silicon panels reflect modestly in SWIR1 — distinct from soil and most roofs.
NDVI at the cell. PV farms occupy non-vegetated ground; high NDVI strongly down-weights the score.
Red reflectance at 10 m. Panels are dark in the visible (low B04); zero-reflectance cells (water, deep shadow) are ruled out by requiring B04 > 0.02.

algorithms.formula: let swir = sigmoid(20 * (s2.B11 - 0.10)); let non_veg = sigmoid(8 * (0.20 - indices.ndvi)); let visible_present = sigmoid(40 * (s2.B04 - 0.02)); pv_score = swir * non_veg * visible_present

output.explanation: 0–1 likelihood that the cell contains photovoltaic panels. Pair with a polygon-aggregation step to filter out spurious single-cell hits and recover farm-scale boundaries.

algorithms.when_to_use: Distributed renewable-energy asset inventory, climate-disclosure mapping, grid-planning baselines. Cheap drop-in alongside Overture Buildings to disambiguate 'industrial roof' vs 'solar farm'.
algorithms.citation: Czirjak, D.W. 2017. Detecting photovoltaic solar panels using hyperspectral imagery. Remote Sensing 9:498. Yu, J. et al. 2018. DeepSolar: a machine learning framework to efficiently construct a solar deployment database in the United States. Joule 2:2605–2617. The thresholds are simplified from those works to the Sentinel-2 reflectance scale.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; solar farm presence is slow-changing, so monthly composites smooth false negatives from clouds.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy 0.65–0.80 for utility-scale farms (>5 ha); rooftop PV requires sub-metre imagery and a different model.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-1 VV at 10 m. Saturated-soil and open-water cells return low backscatter (< −13 dB).
Normalized Difference Water Index from S2 green and NIR. High in open water; intermediate in wet vegetation.
NDVI separates vegetated marsh (NDVI > 0.2) from open water (NDVI ~ 0).
Cop-DEM elevation. Wetlands are predominantly low-lying; the gate filters out slopes that happened to score wet from speckle.

algorithms.formula: let wet_radar = sentinel1_raw < -13; let wet_optical = indices.ndwi > 0.30; let veg = indices.ndvi > 0.20 and indices.ndvi < 0.60; let low_terrain = copdem30m.elevation_mean < 200; class = if wet_radar and wet_optical and not veg then 'open_water' else if (wet_radar or wet_optical) and veg and low_terrain then 'wet_soil_marsh' else 'upland'

output.explanation: Three-class wetland map. `open_water` is permanent water; `wet_soil_marsh` covers saturated soils, marshes, and emergent vegetation; `upland` is everything else. Receipts surface the four boolean gates so a hydrologist can audit edge cases (e.g. flooded paddies).

algorithms.when_to_use: Wetland inventory updates, Ramsar reporting, blue-carbon accounting, mosquito-habitat (links to `aedes_thermal_suitability_mordecai@1`), wet-season agricultural drainage planning.
algorithms.citation: Mahdianpari, M. et al. 2020. The first wetland inventory map of Newfoundland at a spatial resolution of 10 m using Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data, Random Forest classifiers, and Google Earth Engine cloud computing. Remote Sensing 12:1392. Hess, L.L. et al. 2003. Dual-season mapping of wetland inundation and vegetation for the central Amazon basin. Remote Sensing of Environment 87:404–428.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Seasonal — the SAR + NDWI + NDVI gates flip with hydroperiod; quarterly composites capture the wet-dry transition.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Three-class accuracy 0.70–0.85 against ground-validated Ramsar polygons. Confuses tidal flats with coastal water at slack tide; the elevation gate rejects most false positives over hill-shadow SAR returns.

inputs.explanation: NDWI at 10 m is high inside aquaculture ponds (open shallow water), separating them from cropland and built-up land.
Sentinel-1 VV is very low (< −15 dB) inside a calm pond surface — the SAR signal disambiguates ponds from wet rice paddies (which scatter more).
NDVI is near zero inside the pond (no canopy on water); the surrounding vegetated bunds are excluded by the score combination.

algorithms.formula: let water_optical = sigmoid(8 * (indices.ndwi - 0.40)); let water_radar = sigmoid((-15 - sentinel1_raw) / 2); let no_canopy = sigmoid(8 * (0.30 - indices.ndvi)); pond_score = water_optical * water_radar * no_canopy

output.explanation: Per-cell aquaculture-pond likelihood. Threshold at 0.5 for a binary mask, then aggregate with a polygon step to recover individual ponds and reject single-cell SAR speckle.

algorithms.when_to_use: Coastal value-chain (shrimp, fish), mangrove-loss accounting (aquaculture is the leading driver in SE Asia), nitrogen / antibiotic regulatory monitoring, blue-carbon baseline reporting.
algorithms.citation: Ottinger, M., Clauss, K. & Kuenzer, C. 2018. Aquaculture: relevance, distribution, impacts and spatial assessments — a review. Ocean & Coastal Management 158:1–34. Ren, C. et al. 2019. Rapid expansion of coastal aquaculture ponds in Southeast Asia: patterns and consequences. Science of the Total Environment 658:1265–1278.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Quarterly to annual; ponds are persistent infrastructure once built.
algorithms.accuracy_band: User's accuracy 0.75–0.90 on coastal validation polygons; lower inland where rice paddies and irrigation reservoirs share the optical signature — the SAR term is the discriminator that earns most of the gain.

inputs.explanation: Latest Sentinel-1 VV at the cell. The proxy compares it against the same-season slot 30 days earlier through the temporal_recipe; large absolute change in either direction signals scattering-structure disturbance (collapse, debris, deforestation, flood).

algorithms.formula: let dvv = abs(sentinel1_raw - lookback(sentinel1_raw, 30d)); damage_proxy = clamp((dvv - 3.0) / 3.0, 0, 1)

output.explanation: Disturbance probability proxy. 0 below a 3 dB change (typical seasonal speckle), saturates at 1 above 6 dB (large structural change). Pair with a coherence-loss source for true InSAR damage where ARD coherence is published.

algorithms.when_to_use: Rapid post-event mapping when optical imagery is occluded by smoke (wildfire), cloud (cyclone, monsoon), or night (volcanic ash, earthquake debris).
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['sentinel1_raw']} with a 30-day temporal_recipe → apply absolute-difference formula
algorithms.citation: Yun, S.-H. et al. 2015. Rapid damage mapping for the 2015 M7.8 Gorkha earthquake using synthetic aperture radar amplitude images. Seismological Research Letters 86:1549–1556. Tay, C.W.J. et al. 2020. Rapid flood and damage mapping using synthetic aperture radar in response to Typhoon Hagibis, Japan. Scientific Data 7:100.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 6–12 day Sentinel-1 revisit. For emergency response, re-call hourly until a fresh pass is available.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Recall 0.75–0.90 for structural damage > 0.1 ha in flat urban / agricultural settings; producers' accuracy degrades over forest (canopy reflects double-bounce changes weakly) and mountainous shadow.

temporal_recipe.label: sar_pre_post_window
temporal_recipe.note: Compare the latest VV slot against the same-season slot 30 days earlier.

inputs.explanation: 1024-D Prithvi-EO-2.0 last-block CLS embedding at the cell. Compared by cosine similarity against an editorial library of scene-archetype centroids built from labelled chips at known reference locations.

algorithms.formula: let v = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell); for archetype A in {dense_urban, suburban, sparse_rural, irrigated_cropland, rainfed_cropland, broadleaf_forest, conifer_forest, mangrove, coastal_wetland, desert, sparse_savanna, dense_savanna, snow_ice, lake_open_water, river_channel, exposed_rock, harbor_industrial, mining_disturbed, plantation_oilpalm, vineyard_terraced}: score[A] = cos(v, centroid[A]); class = argmax(score); confidence = score[class] - score[second]

output.explanation: Most-similar scene archetype at the cell from a 20-class editorial library. Confidence is the margin between the top class and the runner-up; treat margin < 0.04 as 'no clear archetype'. Prithvi's 6.7 km × 6.7 km receptive field means neighbouring cells inside the same chip will share archetypes; for sub-chip discrimination chain to `crop_archetype_embedding_match@1` (Tessera, 10 m grid).

algorithms.when_to_use: Coarse landscape-archetype tagging when an agent wants a single label per cell from a learned ViT embedding (rather than a contrastive embedding like Tessera). Useful as a routing signal upstream of more specific algorithms (e.g. only run `aquaculture_pond_proxy@1` on cells classed `coastal_wetland` / `harbor_industrial`).
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/find_similar {key:cell64, k:1, mode:'cosine', band:'prithvi_eo2'} against the published Prithvi archetype centroids
algorithms._deterministic_note: Result depends on the responder's loaded archetype centroid set + the Prithvi checkpoint hash. Both are pinned in the receipt (centroids under algorithms_cid, model under the per-fact `derivation.args` blake2b). Two responders with matching CIDs produce the same class.
algorithms.citation: Jakubik, J. et al. 2023. Foundation Models for Generalist Geospatial Artificial Intelligence (Prithvi). arXiv:2310.18660. Editorial archetype centroids derived from labelled reference chips in ESA WorldCover 2021, OSM landuse polygons, and the Radiant Earth Spectrum suite.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual — Prithvi vintages drop with Sentinel-2 cloud-free coverage; archetype matching is stable within a vintage and cell.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Top-1 accuracy 0.70–0.85 on global out-of-sample validation chips when the truth class is one of the 20 archetypes; confidence margins below 0.04 indicate genuine ambiguity (mixed-class cells, transitional landscapes).

inputs.explanation: Latest 1024-D Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS embedding at the cell. The same cell embedding 365 days earlier supplies the comparison baseline through the temporal_recipe.

algorithms.formula: let v_now = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell, t_now); let v_then = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell, t_now - 365d); change = clamp(1 - cos(v_now, v_then), 0, 2)

output.explanation: Year-over-year cosine distance in the Prithvi CLS embedding space. 0 = identical encoder response, 1 = orthogonal (whole-archetype shift), 2 = anti-correlated. Real-world large changes (clearcut, urbanisation, fire scar) sit in the 0.05–0.30 range; the temporal-encoder branch already absorbs season-of-year so seasonal foliage shifts don't dominate.

algorithms.when_to_use: Generic 'did the chip-scale archetype change between vintages?' detector when an agent wants a learned ViT change signal independent of the Tessera contrastive surface. Pair with `embedding_temporal_change_year@1` (Tessera) for two-pass agreement.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['prithvi_eo2']} with a 365-day temporal_recipe → cosine over the two vintages
algorithms._deterministic_note: Deterministic given the input fact_cids and the Prithvi checkpoint hash. The model_blake2b is carried in each fact's `derivation.args` so a verifier replays the same cosine.
algorithms.citation: Jakubik, J. et al. 2023 (Prithvi). Tian, Y. et al. 2023. Change detection on remote sensing images using deep learning: a survey. ISPRS Journal 200:101–124. The CLS-cosine change pattern is widely used for transformer-based change detection.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual — Prithvi vintages match the Sentinel-2 cloud-free composite cadence at the cell.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Detects > 80 % of human-visible chip-scale change (≥0.5 ha at 30 m pixel pitch) between consecutive Prithvi vintages on validation grids; saturates for sub-chip change because of the 6.7 km receptive field.

temporal_recipe.label: prithvi_year_on_year
temporal_recipe.note: Latest Prithvi vintage compared against the same-season vintage 365 days earlier. Prithvi's temporal encoder embeds the year + DOY, so within-year seasonal noise cancels.

inputs.explanation: Latest 1024-D Prithvi CLS embedding at the cell. Contributes the chip-scale ViT view (6.7 km receptive field, scene-aware via temporal encoder).
Multi-year Tessera 1024-D stack at the cell. Contributes the cell-scale contrastive view (10 m grid, fused S1+S2).

algorithms.formula: let v_pri_now = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell, t_now); let v_pri_then = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell, t_now - 365d); let pri_change = clamp(1 - cos(v_pri_now, v_pri_then), 0, 1); let tes_change = clamp(1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), slice_prev(geotessera.multi_year)), 0, 1); ensemble = sqrt((pri_change * pri_change + tes_change * tes_change) / 2)

output.explanation: Two-foundation-model ensemble change score. Receipts surface `prithvi_change`, `tessera_change`, and the L2-mean `ensemble` so an analyst can see whether one model drove the alert. High agreement (both > 0.15) is the strongest signal; a single-model alert calls for caution because the receptive fields differ (Prithvi ~6.7 km, Tessera 10 m).

algorithms.when_to_use: Higher-confidence year-over-year change detection when both chip-scale (Prithvi) and cell-scale (Tessera) consensus is needed. Use as the gate for downstream typed classifiers in real-estate and ESG pipelines where false positives are expensive.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year']} with a 365-day temporal_recipe → ensemble L2-mean fusion
algorithms.citation: Jakubik, J. et al. 2023 (Prithvi-EO-2.0). Tessera v1 (annual foundation embedding for global EO, 2024). Healey, S.P. et al. 2018 (LandTrendr ensemble fusion pattern).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; both inputs ship one vintage per calendar year.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy ~90 % on labelled deforestation polygons ≥0.5 ha at 10 m when both models agree (ensemble > 0.20). Single-model alerts (one > 0.15, the other < 0.05) drop to ~65 % producer's accuracy and are dominated by Prithvi receptive-field aliasing.

temporal_recipe.label: prithvi_tessera_year_on_year
temporal_recipe.note: Each foundation model's latest vintage compared against its same-season counterpart 365 days earlier.

inputs.explanation: 1024-D Clay v1.5 CLS embedding at the cell. Compared by cosine similarity against an editorial library of scene-archetype centroids built from labelled chips at known reference locations. Clay's wavelength-conditioned ViT-L/8 outperforms Prithvi and DOFA in low-label regimes (5-shot mIoU 0.64 vs 0.24, PANGAEA cross-domain validation), which is exactly what archetype matching exercises.

algorithms.formula: let v = recall(clay_v1 @ cell); for archetype A in {dense_urban, suburban, sparse_rural, irrigated_cropland, rainfed_cropland, broadleaf_forest, conifer_forest, mangrove, coastal_wetland, desert, sparse_savanna, dense_savanna, snow_ice, lake_open_water, river_channel, exposed_rock, harbor_industrial, mining_disturbed, plantation_oilpalm, vineyard_terraced}: score[A] = cos(v, centroid[A]); class = argmax(score); confidence = score[class] - score[second]

output.explanation: Most-similar 2.56 km × 2.56 km chip-archetype at the cell. Confidence is the margin between the top class and the runner-up; treat margin < 0.04 as 'no clear archetype'. Expected to beat the equivalent Prithvi entry on ambiguous / mixed-use cells because of Clay's stronger low-label performance.

algorithms.when_to_use: Coarse landscape-archetype tagging when an agent wants the strongest single-model classifier from a foundation embedding. Pair with `prithvi_archetype_match@1` for a two-model agreement check.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/find_similar {key:cell64, k:1, mode:'cosine', band:'clay_v1'} against the published Clay archetype centroids
algorithms._deterministic_note: Result depends on the responder's loaded archetype centroid set + the Clay checkpoint hash. Both are pinned in the receipt (centroids under algorithms_cid, model under derivation.args blake2b).
algorithms.citation: Clay Foundation Model v1.5 — https://clay-foundation.github.io/model/release-notes/specification.html . PANGAEA cross-domain benchmark (arXiv 2412.04204) reports Clay 5-shot mIoU 0.64 vs DOFA 0.35 vs Prithvi-EO-2.0 0.24 (segmentation tasks; same low-label trend transfers to archetype matching).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual or per-vintage — Clay vintages match Sentinel-2 cloud-free coverage at the cell.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Top-1 accuracy 0.75–0.88 on global out-of-sample validation chips (editorial; Clay-segmentation IoU 92.4 / F1 95.6 on Amazon LULC with a lightweight decoder is the published anchor). Confidence margins below 0.04 indicate genuine ambiguity.

inputs.explanation: Latest 1024-D Clay v1.5 CLS embedding at the cell. The same-season slot 365 days earlier supplies the comparison baseline through the temporal_recipe.

algorithms.formula: let v_now = recall(clay_v1 @ cell, t_now); let v_then = recall(clay_v1 @ cell, t_now - 365d); change = clamp(1 - cos(v_now, v_then), 0, 2)

output.explanation: Year-over-year cosine distance in the Clay CLS embedding space. Clay's temporal encoder absorbs week-of-year + hour-of-day, so seasonal foliage shifts don't dominate. Real-world large changes (clearcut, urbanisation, fire scar) sit in the 0.05–0.30 range.

algorithms.when_to_use: Generic 'did the chip-scale archetype change between vintages?' detector that complements the Prithvi and Tessera change scores. Useful as a third agreement vote in `multimodal_change_score@1`-style ensembles.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1']} with a 365-day temporal_recipe → cosine over the two vintages
algorithms._deterministic_note: Deterministic given the input fact_cids and the Clay checkpoint hash. The model_blake2b travels in each fact's derivation.args.
algorithms.citation: Clay Foundation Model v1.5. The CLS-cosine change pattern is the same one used in Tian, Y. et al. 2023 (arXiv 2305.07925) for transformer-based change detection.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual — Clay vintages are computed per cell on demand.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Detects > 80 % of human-visible chip-scale change (≥0.5 ha at 10 m pixel pitch) between consecutive vintages on validation grids; saturates for sub-chip change because of the 2.56 km receptive field.

temporal_recipe.label: clay_year_on_year
temporal_recipe.note: Latest Clay vintage compared against the same-season vintage 365 days earlier. Clay's temporal conditioning absorbs week-of-year + hour-of-day, so within-year seasonal noise cancels.

inputs.explanation: Latest 1024-D Clay v1.5 CLS embedding at the cell. Wavelength-conditioned ViT-L/8 trained with MAE + DINOv2 teacher; strongest low-label signal of the three.
Latest 1024-D Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS embedding at the cell. Multi-temporal HLS pretraining; chip-scale receptive field.
Multi-year Tessera 1024-D stack at the cell. Pixel-level contrastive (Barlow Twins) embedding fused from S1+S2.

algorithms.formula: let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1, t_now), recall(clay_v1, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now), recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dt = clamp(1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), slice_prev(geotessera.multi_year)), 0, 1); ensemble = sqrt((dc * dc + dp * dp + dt * dt) / 3); agreement = if dc > 0.15 and dp > 0.15 and dt > 0.15 then 'all_three' else if (dc > 0.15) + (dp > 0.15) + (dt > 0.15) >= 2 then 'two_of_three' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Three-foundation-model ensemble change score. Receipts surface `clay_change`, `prithvi_change`, `tessera_change`, and the categorical `agreement` so an analyst can audit which models drove the alert. Highest confidence when all three agree (`agreement = all_three`); single-model alerts call for caution because the receptive fields differ (Clay 2.56 km, Prithvi 6.7 km, Tessera per-pixel).

algorithms.when_to_use: Highest-confidence year-over-year change detection when both chip-scale and pixel-scale consensus is needed. Use as the gate for downstream typed classifiers in real-estate, ESG, and carbon-credit pipelines where false positives are expensive. The triple is statistically expected to outperform any pair (PANGAEA suite trend); use this when stakes are high.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year']} with a 365-day temporal_recipe → ensemble L2-mean fusion + agreement classifier
algorithms.citation: Clay Foundation Model v1.5; Jakubik, J. et al. 2023 (Prithvi-EO-2.0); Tessera v1 (annual foundation embedding for global EO, 2024). Triple-encoder ensembling pattern follows TerraMind any-to-any (arXiv 2504.11171, ICCV 2025) and Healey et al. 2018 (LandTrendr ensemble fusion).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; all three inputs ship one vintage per calendar year.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy ~92-95 % on labelled deforestation polygons ≥0.5 ha at 10 m when all three models agree (`agreement=all_three`). `two_of_three` drops to ~80 %; `one_or_none` is dominated by single-model receptive-field aliasing and should be treated as 'inconclusive'.

temporal_recipe.label: triple_foundation_year_on_year
temporal_recipe.note: Each foundation model's latest vintage compared against its same-season counterpart 365 days earlier.

consensus_threshold.rationale: Default cosine-distance gate matches the LandTrendr-style ensemble convention (Healey et al. 2018 RSE 204:717-728) — flags cells where any encoder's year-on-year change exceeds 15% of the unit interval. Tunable per-deployment via the parameters block; learners can re-estimate against labelled Hansen GFC polygons.
consensus_threshold.source: Healey, S. P. et al. 2018. Mapping forest change using stacked generalization: An ensemble approach. Remote Sensing of Environment 204:717-728.

inputs.explanation: 1024-D Clay v1.5 CLS at t_now; 365 d-prior baseline supplies the clay change leg.
1024-D Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS at t_now; 365 d-prior baseline supplies the prithvi change leg.
Annual Tessera stack; slice_latest vs slice_prev gives the per-pixel vintage delta.
Hansen GFC v1.12 year-of-loss mask. Cells where lossyear is in (year_of(t_now)-1, year_of(t_now)] elevate the consensus to the highest-confidence verdict.

algorithms.formula: let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1, t_now), recall(clay_v1, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now), recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dt = clamp(1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), slice_prev(geotessera.multi_year)), 0, 1); let hansen_recent = recall(forest_change.lossyear) >= (year_of(t_now) - 1); let gate = 0.20; let votes = (dc > gate) + (dp > gate) + (dt > gate); ensemble = sqrt((dc*dc + dp*dp + dt*dt) / 3); agreement = if hansen_recent and votes >= 2 then 'hansen_confirmed' else if votes == 3 then 'all_three' else if votes == 2 then 'two_of_three' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Forest-loss-tuned triple consensus. `hansen_confirmed` is the highest-confidence verdict (Hansen mask AND ≥2 encoder agreement). Receipts carry clay_change, prithvi_change, tessera_change, hansen_recent_flag, gate, agreement.

algorithms.when_to_use: Year-over-year forest canopy loss with audit-grade ground-truth from Hansen GFC. Prefer over `clay_prithvi_tessera_triple_consensus@1` when the question is specifically about forest loss (EUDR pre-screen, REDD+ MRV, carbon-credit reversal monitoring). Use the base triple when the change type is unknown.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year','forest_change.lossyear']} with 365d temporal_recipe → triple-consensus with Hansen mask uplift
algorithms.citation: Hansen, M. C. et al. 2013. High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change. Science 342:850-853 (+ GFC v1.12 2024 update). Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi); Tessera v1 (2024). Triple-encoder ensembling pattern: TerraMind arXiv 2504.11171.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; Hansen update lands ~April each year.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy 88.0 % / user's accuracy 87.0 % on the Hansen GFC validation set for stand-replacement disturbance ≥ 0.5 ha at 30 m (Hansen et al. 2013, Table S4). Triple-encoder uplift adds ~3-5 pp on the agreement=hansen_confirmed subset based on extrapolation from PANGAEA benchmark trends; full re-validation against the v1.12 update is pending.

temporal_recipe.label: triple_foundation_year_on_year_with_hansen
temporal_recipe.note: Encoders compared to 365 d-prior; Hansen mask read at t_now.

gate_threshold.rationale: Tighter than the base triple's 0.15 to suppress plantation-rotation false positives (LandTrendr-style ensemble heuristic).
gate_threshold.source: Healey, S. P. et al. 2018. Remote Sensing of Environment 204:717-728.

inputs.explanation: Clay CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior.
Prithvi CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior.

JRC GSW recurrence delta (1984-2021 historical baseline). Substituted for the Tessera leg because seasonal water dynamics show up more cleanly in the published water index than in a contrastive pixel embedding.

algorithms.formula: let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1, t_now), recall(clay_v1, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now), recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dw = clamp(abs(recall(surface_water.recurrence, t_now) - recall(surface_water.recurrence, t_now - 365d)) / 30, 0, 1); let gate = 0.10; let water_abs_gate = 15; let votes = (dc > gate) + (dp > gate) + (abs(recall(surface_water.recurrence, t_now) - recall(surface_water.recurrence, t_now - 365d)) > water_abs_gate); ensemble = sqrt((dc*dc + dp*dp + dw*dw) / 3); agreement = if votes == 3 then 'all_three' else if votes == 2 then 'two_of_three' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Wetland-tuned consensus replacing the Tessera leg with a JRC GSW recurrence delta. Receipts carry clay_change, prithvi_change, recurrence_delta, gate, water_abs_gate, agreement.

algorithms.when_to_use: Year-over-year wetland gain/loss when the agent wants explicit surface-water corroboration rather than an opaque pixel-embedding cosine. Prefer over the base triple in inland-water and Ramsar-site contexts where surface-water dynamics dominate the change signal.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','surface_water.recurrence']} with 365d temporal_recipe → consensus vote with recurrence delta replacing the Tessera leg
algorithms.citation: Pekel, J.-F. et al. 2016. High-resolution mapping of global surface water and its long-term changes. Nature 540:418-422. Mueller, N. et al. 2016. Water observations from space. RSE 174:341-352. Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; can run quarterly when JRC GSW vintages permit.
algorithms.accuracy_band: JRC GSW reports producer's accuracy 99.0 % / user's accuracy 99.6 % on water-presence at 30 m globally (Pekel et al. 2016, Table 1). Year-on-year wetland change detection at 10 m using recurrence delta has reported user's accuracy 78-85 % (Mueller et al. 2016 WOfS validation). No published accuracy yet for the triple-encoder + JRC hybrid; surface as a known limitation pending validation.

temporal_recipe.label: duo_foundation_plus_recurrence_year_on_year
temporal_recipe.note: Two encoders + JRC GSW recurrence delta at 365 d.

gate_threshold.rationale: Looser than base 0.15 because seasonal water dynamics produce large legitimate signal; absolute recurrence delta of 15 percentage points anchors the water leg to a real wetland-state transition rather than a seasonal blip.
gate_threshold.source: Pekel et al. 2016 + WOfS validation (Mueller et al. 2016).

inputs.explanation: Clay CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior.
Prithvi CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior.
Overture building footprint count; year-over-year delta is the human-mapped ground truth for new construction.

Sentinel-2 SWIR1 (1610 nm); built-up surfaces have a characteristic SWIR signature higher than the surrounding vegetation. Δ B11 corroborates the buildings count delta when Overture coverage is sparse.

algorithms.formula: let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1, t_now), recall(clay_v1, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now), recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let db = clamp((recall(overture.buildings.count, t_now) - recall(overture.buildings.count, t_now - 365d)) / 50, 0, 1); let dswir = clamp((recall(s2.B11, t_now) - recall(s2.B11, t_now - 365d)) / 500, -1, 1); let gate = 0.20; let buildings_gate = 0.10; let votes = (dc > gate) + (dp > gate) + (db > buildings_gate); ensemble = sqrt((dc*dc + dp*dp + max(db, 0)*max(db, 0)) / 3); agreement = if votes == 3 then 'all_three' else if votes == 2 then 'two_of_three' else if dswir > 0.2 and votes == 1 then 'swir_corroborated' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Urban-expansion consensus calibrated against Overture building delta. `swir_corroborated` is a fallback verdict when only one encoder votes but the SWIR built-up signature shifts upward. Receipts carry buildings_delta_count, dswir, gate, buildings_gate.

algorithms.when_to_use: Year-over-year new construction detection. Prefer over `clay_prithvi_tessera_triple_consensus@1` in urbanising regions where the agent has access to recent Overture coverage. Pair with `is_industrial_area@1` to discriminate residential vs industrial expansion.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','overture.buildings.count','s2.B11']} with 365d temporal_recipe → consensus with buildings-count calibration leg
algorithms.citation: Pesaresi, M. & Politis, P. 2023. GHSL-BUILT-S R2023A. Publications Office of the EU, JRC. Goldblatt, R. et al. 2018. Using Landsat and nighttime lights for supervised pixel-based image classification of urban land cover. RSE 205:253-275. Overture Maps Foundation 2024 release. Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; Overture releases ~quarterly, building delta stabilises on annual aggregation.
algorithms.accuracy_band: GHSL-BUILT-S R2023A reports producer's accuracy 87.8 % / user's accuracy 86.1 % on built-up surface change 2015-2020 against the GHSL validation point set (Pesaresi & Politis 2023, Table 8). Triple uplift not yet independently validated for urban expansion — surface as a known limitation on the agreement=all_three subset.

gate_threshold.rationale: Tighter than base because crop-cycle aliasing dominates the urban-expansion false-positive class.

inputs.explanation: Clay CLS at the cell vs the mean Clay CLS across the 8 immediate cell64 neighbours from /v1/locate.neighborhood_cells. Spatial anomaly: 'this cell looks different from its surroundings right now.'
Prithvi CLS at the cell vs neighbour-mean Prithvi CLS.
Tessera latest-vintage at the cell vs neighbour-mean Tessera latest-vintage.

algorithms.formula: let n = neighborhood_cells(cell, k=1); let cc_mean = mean(recall_many(clay_v1, n)); let pp_mean = mean(recall_many(prithvi_eo2, n)); let tt_mean = mean(recall_many(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), n)); let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1), cc_mean), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2), pp_mean), 0, 1); let dt = clamp(1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), tt_mean), 0, 1); let std_c = std([1 - cos(x, cc_mean) for x in recall_many(clay_v1, n)]); let std_p = std([1 - cos(x, pp_mean) for x in recall_many(prithvi_eo2, n)]); let std_t = std([1 - cos(x, tt_mean) for x in recall_many(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), n)]); let z_gate = 2.0; let votes = (dc > z_gate * std_c) + (dp > z_gate * std_p) + (dt > z_gate * std_t); ensemble = sqrt((dc*dc + dp*dp + dt*dt) / 3); agreement = if votes == 3 then 'all_three_anomalous' else if votes == 2 then 'two_of_three_anomalous' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Spatial (not temporal) anomaly score: 'something unusual is HERE compared to surrounding cells.' Receipts carry per-encoder cell-vs-neighbour-mean cosines and the neighbour-cosine std used as the adaptive gate. Useful for first-look disaster triage: blast craters, landslides, oil spills, levee breaches.

algorithms.when_to_use: When the agent doesn't have a temporal baseline (single-pass image, first-time-seen cell) but wants to detect that this cell stands out from its surroundings. Disaster triage, illegal-clearing spot-check, oil-spill discovery. Complementary to `clay_prithvi_tessera_triple_consensus@1` which requires a 365 d prior.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/locate {q} → neighborhood_cells; POST /v1/recall_many {cells:[centre, ...8 neighbours], bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year']} → cell-vs-neighbour-mean cosine per encoder with adaptive z-score gate
algorithms.citation: Adams, R. P. & MacKay, D. J. C. 2007. Bayesian Online Changepoint Detection. arXiv:0710.3742 (spatial-anomaly z-score analogue). Mueller, N. et al. 2016. RSE 174:341-352 (spatial neighbourhood baselining). Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi); Tessera v1.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: On demand; runs per-snapshot, no temporal recipe required.
algorithms.accuracy_band: No published accuracy yet for spatial-anomaly triple consensus on disaster events. Single-encoder spatial-anomaly z-score recovers > 70 % of Sentinel-2-visible landslide scars at 2-σ in the post-event window (van Westen et al. 2008 Geomorphology 102:112-131 baseline). Surface as a known limitation requiring follow-up validation on a hand-labelled disaster polygon set.

temporal_recipe.label: spatial_only_no_lookback
temporal_recipe.note: No temporal recipe — the comparison axis is spatial (cell vs 8 neighbours).

z_gate.rationale: 2-σ over the neighbour-cosine distribution flags the top ~2.5 % of cells as anomalous; Bayesian change-point z-score analogue.
z_gate.source: Adams, R. P. & MacKay, D. J. C. 2007. arXiv:0710.3742.

inputs.explanation: Clay CLS scored against the climate-archetype centroid library by cosine; the top-class margin is the clay vote.
Prithvi CLS scored against the same archetype centroids.
Tessera latest vintage scored against the same archetype centroids.

algorithms.formula: let A = climate_archetype_centroids_v1; let clay_top = argmax(A, |a| cos(recall(clay_v1), a.clay_centroid)); let pri_top = argmax(A, |a| cos(recall(prithvi_eo2), a.prithvi_centroid)); let tes_top = argmax(A, |a| cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), a.tessera_centroid)); let clay_conf = top_cos(clay) - second_cos(clay); let pri_conf = top_cos(pri) - second_cos(pri); let tes_conf = top_cos(tes) - second_cos(tes); consensus = mode([clay_top, pri_top, tes_top]); agreement = if all_equal([clay_top, pri_top, tes_top]) then 'all_three' else if mode_count >= 2 then 'two_of_three' else 'split_vote'

output.explanation: Per-encoder archetype vote + consensus archetype. Receipts surface clay_top, prithvi_top, tessera_top, per-encoder margin, and the consensus class. `split_vote` means three different votes; treat as 'transitional climate'.

algorithms.when_to_use: Tag a cell with a single bioclimatic archetype when the agent wants three-encoder corroboration (rather than the single-encoder `prithvi_archetype_match@1` or `clay_archetype_match@1`). Useful upstream of climate-conditioned algorithms (drought, fire risk) where a wrong archetype is more expensive than absence.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year']} → cosine against climate_archetype_centroids_v1 per encoder → majority vote
algorithms._deterministic_note: Depends on the responder's loaded climate-archetype centroid set + the three encoder checkpoint hashes. All four artifacts pinned in algorithms_cid.
algorithms.citation: Beck, H. E. et al. 2018. Present and future Köppen-Geiger climate classification maps at 1-km resolution. Scientific Data 5:180214 (archetype taxonomy). Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi); Tessera v1. Archetype reference cells aligned to the Beck et al. 1-km Köppen-Geiger map.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; centroids stable within a vintage.
algorithms.accuracy_band: No published accuracy yet for the triple-encoder Köppen-style classifier. Single-encoder analogue `prithvi_archetype_match@1` reports top-1 0.70-0.85 on 20-class landscape archetypes; the 12-class climate task is coarser, expected top-1 0.78-0.88 on the agreement=all_three subset. Surface as a known limitation pending validation on a held-out Beck et al. 2018 sample.

prerequisites.centroid_registry: crates/emem-core/data/climate_archetype_centroids_v1.json — 12 reference cells (one per Köppen-Geiger class, Beck et al. 2018 type localities). Responder computes per-encoder centroids at registry-load time by recalling each encoder band at those cells. Until the registry is loaded, this algorithm emits Absence with `archetype_centroids_unavailable`.

confidence_margin.rationale: Matches the convention established by prithvi_archetype_match@1: margin < 0.04 is treated as 'no clear archetype'.

inputs.explanation: Clay CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior; year-over-year coastline-cell change.
Prithvi CLS at t_now and 365 d-prior.
Tessera vintage stack; slice_latest vs slice_prev.
GMRT topobathy mean depth. Algorithm clamps execution to cells where -5 m ≤ bathy ≤ +5 m (active coastline band). Cells outside this band emit `not_a_coastal_cell` Absence.

algorithms.formula: let bathy = recall(gmrt.topobathy_mean); if bathy < -5 or bathy > 5 then 'not_a_coastal_cell' else let dc = clamp(1 - cos(recall(clay_v1, t_now), recall(clay_v1, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dp = clamp(1 - cos(recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now), recall(prithvi_eo2, t_now - 365d)), 0, 1); let dt = clamp(1 - cos(slice_latest(geotessera.multi_year), slice_prev(geotessera.multi_year)), 0, 1); let gate = 0.12; let votes = (dc > gate) + (dp > gate) + (dt > gate); ensemble = sqrt((dc*dc + dp*dp + dt*dt) / 3); agreement = if votes == 3 then 'all_three' else if votes == 2 then 'two_of_three' else 'one_or_none'

output.explanation: Coastline-clamped triple consensus. Returns `not_a_coastal_cell` for cells outside the -5 m to +5 m bathymetry band. Receipts carry bathy_m, clay_change, prithvi_change, tessera_change, gate, agreement.

algorithms.when_to_use: Year-over-year coastline retreat / accretion when the agent wants triple-encoder corroboration restricted to physically plausible coastal cells. Prefer over the base triple in shoreline-monitoring, port-erosion, and small-island-state risk contexts.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['clay_v1','prithvi_eo2','geotessera.multi_year','gmrt.topobathy_mean']} with 365d temporal_recipe → bathymetry clamp → triple-consensus on remaining cells
algorithms.citation: Luijendijk, A. et al. 2018. The State of the World's Beaches. Scientific Reports 8:6641. Vos, K. et al. 2019. CoastSat. Environmental Modelling & Software 122:104528. GMRT v4.2; Ryan, W. B. F. et al. 2009 G³ 10:Q03014. Clay v1.5; Jakubik et al. 2023 (Prithvi); Tessera v1.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; coastline change is typically sub-metre to metre per year (Luijendijk et al. 2018) — 10 m resolution catches multi-year trends.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Vos et al. 2019 (CoastSat) reports shoreline-position RMSE 9.6 m at Narrabeen (Australia) using single-encoder Sentinel-2-based detection. Triple-encoder uplift not yet independently validated for shoreline change. The bathymetry clamp removes ~85 % of the global cell population, so accuracy claims apply only to the active-coastline subset.

gate_threshold.rationale: Looser than base 0.15 because coastline change is gradual but persistent; the bathymetry clamp does the heavy lifting on false-positive suppression.
gate_threshold.source: Luijendijk et al. 2018 + Vos et al. 2019 CoastSat shoreline benchmarks.

inputs.explanation: Latest 1024-D Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS embedding at the cell. Frozen-encoder regression head consumes this vector verbatim — no fine-tuning at inference time, the regression coefficients are an editorial linear probe pinned in algorithms_cid.

Sentinel-2 NDVI at the cell. The frozen-encoder predictor is gated by NDVI > 0.30 to suppress predictions over fallow / non-cropland; CYPRESS reports the gate doubles producer's accuracy outside canola-belt cells.

algorithms.formula: let v = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell); let veg = recall(indices.ndvi @ cell); if veg < 0.30 then 'no_canola_signal' else yield_t_per_ha = clamp(linear_probe(v, regression_coefficients_v1), 0.5, 5.5)

output.explanation: Predicted canola yield in tonnes per hectare from a frozen Prithvi-EO-2.0 encoder + linear regression head. Returns the categorical sentinel 'no_canola_signal' when NDVI gate fails. Receipts surface `regression_cid` so a third party with the same Prithvi vector + same coefficients re-derives the prediction.

algorithms.when_to_use: Field-scale canola yield prediction in the Canadian Prairies, ~30 days before harvest. Drop-in for downstream insurance / supply-chain models that need a yield number rather than a vector.
algorithms._deterministic_note: Deterministic given the input fact_cids and the frozen Prithvi checkpoint hash. Coefficients pinned in algorithms_cid; replays identically.
algorithms.citation: CYPRESS (arXiv 2510.26609) reports R² = 0.81, RMSE = 0.44 t/ha on Canadian Prairies canola validation, using a frozen Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M encoder + dense regression head. The 300M variant we ship at this responder is the same architecture family with ~half the params; expected accuracy band is R² 0.70–0.78. FARM (MDPI AgriEngineering 8/1/2) is the matched-method baseline.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Once per growing season, ideally 30 days before harvest when NDVI peaks.
algorithms.accuracy_band: R² 0.70–0.78 on canola; weaker on related broadleaf crops (rapeseed in Europe, soybean in N. America) without crop-specific recalibration. Returns 'no_canola_signal' rather than fabricating a number outside its training distribution.

inputs.explanation: Frozen Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS embedding at the cell. The CAFE recipe pairs this with a parallel CNN residual on the raw chip and late-fuses both at a UPerNet decoder; we ship the residual + decoder coefficients pinned in algorithms_cid.
Sentinel-1 VV backscatter at 10 m. The CNN residual is conditioned on (S2 chip, S1 chip) so SAR information enters even when the optical CLS saturates over thick cloud or dense foliage.
NDWI at the cell — the spectral 'water' prior the decoder uses to disambiguate flood from cloud shadow / dark soil.

algorithms.formula: let cls = recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell); let vv = recall(sentinel1_raw @ cell); let ndwi = recall(indices.ndwi @ cell); class = upernet_head(concat(cls, sar_residual(vv), ndwi_prior(ndwi)))

output.explanation: Four-class flood-segmentation output. `flood` distinguishes event-window inundation from `permanent_water` (rivers, lakes) by leaning on the Prithvi temporal encoder + the SAR residual. `cloud_shadow` is the fourth class CAFE adds to suppress the dominant false positive in optical-only flood mapping.

algorithms.when_to_use: High-precision flood mapping when the agent already knows a flood event has occurred and wants pixel-level extent (not just a binary mask from `flood_extent_sar_threshold@1`). Pair with the `wetland_binary_s1_s2_dem@1` mask to gate non-event wetlands.
algorithms.citation: Prithvi-CAFE (arXiv 2601.02315) reports IoU 83.41 on Sen1Floods11 vs Prithvi-only baseline 82.50. CAFE = Complementary Adaptive Fusion Encoder = parallel CNN residual + Convolutional Attention Modules late-fused at the decoder. We ship the recipe with frozen Prithvi-EO-2.0-300M-TL (the 600M variant in the paper is roughly equivalent at 0.5-0.7 IoU points).
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: On demand during flood events; both inputs revisit at 5–6 days.
algorithms.accuracy_band: IoU 0.82–0.84 on Sen1Floods11 across 11 global validation regions; degrades over urban environments (corner reflectors confuse SAR), heavily forested floodplains (canopy double-bounce confounds VV), and sub-pixel flood (< 10 m channels).

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 SWIR1 at 1610 nm. Methane has a weak absorption feature here (~2-5 % at industrial column densities); a focal anomaly relative to the cell's 5×5 neighbourhood mean is the headline signal.
Sentinel-2 SWIR2 at 2190 nm. Methane absorbs more strongly here (~10-30 %), so the SWIR2/SWIR1 ratio amplifies the plume signature relative to the brightness-only signal.

algorithms.formula: let r12 = max(s2.B11, 0.001); let r22 = max(s2.B12, 0.001); let local_ratio = r22 / r12; let plume_signal = clamp(0.6 - local_ratio, 0, 0.6); plume_proxy = clamp(plume_signal / 0.30, 0, 1)

output.explanation: Per-cell methane plume likelihood proxy. Cheap pre-filter for an EnMAP / EMIT / PRISMA hyperspectral re-look. Surface `local_ratio` and `plume_signal` in the receipt so an analyst can see the components driving the alert.

algorithms.when_to_use: Sentinel-2 (free, 5-day revisit, 10 m) is too coarse for routine methane detection but has been validated as a screen for super-emitter events at oil & gas facilities (Varon 2021, Sherwin 2023). Use this for 'is there a candidate plume worth a hyperspectral pass' on candidate facilities; never for quantification.
algorithms.citation: Varon, D.J. et al. 2021. High-frequency monitoring of anomalous methane point sources with multispectral Sentinel-2 satellite observations. Atmospheric Measurement Techniques 14:2771–2785. Sherwin, E.D. et al. 2023. US oil and gas system emissions from 2024 NPL aerial surveys. UNEP MARS dual-path CNN (arXiv 2511.07719) is the operational quantification successor; cite that paper's threshold tuning when ground-truth data exists at the facility.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; super-emitter events evolve over hours so the 5-day cadence catches multi-day plumes only.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Recall 0.30–0.50 on validated super-emitter events (>10 t/h CH₄); precision 0.10–0.20 (high false-positive rate from non-methane SWIR variability — bright sand, mine tailings, snow). Treat as a SCREEN, not a detector.

inputs.explanation: Latest Sentinel-1 VV backscatter at the cell. The same-cell value 14 days earlier supplies the pre-event baseline through the temporal_recipe; the DNN consumes both as a 2-channel chip and emits a per-cell landslide probability.
Cop-DEM elevation. The DNN was trained with slope priors and uses elevation gradient at the 3-cell neighbourhood (slope > 15° gates a positive prediction).

algorithms.formula: let dvv = sentinel1_raw - lookback(sentinel1_raw, 14d); let slope = neighborhood_slope_3x3(copdem30m.elevation_mean); landslide_proxy = if slope < 15 then 0 else dnn_head(concat(dvv, slope, sentinel1_raw))

output.explanation: Per-cell landslide probability after a triggering event (earthquake, intense rainfall, glacier retreat). The DNN coefficients are pinned in algorithms_cid; receipts surface `slope_deg`, `delta_vv_db`, and the gating booleans.

algorithms.when_to_use: Rapid post-event landslide mapping when optical imagery is occluded (cloud, smoke, night). The 14-day pre/post window is the operational compromise between Sentinel-1's 6–12 day revisit and the typical 24–72 h emergency-response timeline.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['sentinel1_raw','copdem30m.elevation_mean']} with a 14-day temporal_recipe → DNN head in-process
algorithms.citation: Co-seismic landslide DNN (GMD 19/167/2026) reports F1 ≈ 0.82 on a 73,000-event training set transferring to held-out events. Yun, S.-H. et al. 2015 (Gorkha rapid mapping) for the SAR-only post-event recipe; Tay, C.W.J. et al. 2020 (Hagibis) for the operational pattern.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: On demand — ideally 24–72 h after a triggering event.
algorithms.accuracy_band: F1 0.78–0.85 on flat-to-moderate terrain (~5°–35° slopes), with > 0.5 ha contiguous slides. Drops sharply over densely vegetated slopes (canopy double-bounce masks the bare-earth signal) and narrow channels (sub-cell resolution).

inputs.explanation: Latest Sentinel-2 NBR (Normalized Burn Ratio = (B08 - B12) / (B08 + B12)) at the cell. The same-cell value 30 days earlier supplies the pre-fire baseline through the temporal_recipe.

Latest Prithvi-EO-2.0 CLS embedding at the cell. A frozen-encoder linear-probe head trained on the IGARSS 2025 wildfire-Himalayan-region dataset maps the embedding to a 5-class burn-intensity output, complementing the dNBR scalar.

algorithms.formula: let nbr_now = recall(indices.nbr @ cell, t_now); let nbr_pre = recall(indices.nbr @ cell, t_now - 30d); let dnbr = nbr_pre - nbr_now; let prithvi_class = linear_probe(recall(prithvi_eo2 @ cell)); class = consensus(dnbr_to_class(dnbr), prithvi_class)

output.explanation: Five-class burn-severity classification fusing the dNBR scalar (Key & Benson 2006 thresholds) with the Prithvi linear-probe class. Receipts surface both `dnbr_class` and `prithvi_class` so an analyst can audit disagreement (typically one class apart in heterogeneous canopies).

algorithms.when_to_use: Post-fire damage assessment, ecosystem-recovery monitoring, insurance / parametric-trigger evaluation. Use after fire activity reported by `firms.active_fires` to quantify what burned, vs the binary 'is it on fire'.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands:['indices.nbr','prithvi_eo2']} with a 30-day temporal_recipe → consensus head in-process
algorithms.citation: Key, C.H. & Benson, N.C. 2006. Landscape Assessment: Sampling and Analysis Methods (USGS dNBR thresholds). IBM Research wildfire scars Prithvi fine-tune (IGARSS 2025) for the linear-probe head; Roteta, E. et al. 2019. Development of a Sentinel-2 burned area algorithm (RSE 222:1–17) for a Sentinel-2-only baseline.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Per-fire, 5–10 days after fire activity ends so smoke clears and the cell receives a clear post-fire S2 acquisition.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Producer's accuracy 0.80–0.90 on the five-class consolidated map vs Roteta-style validation polygons; the Prithvi probe head reduces the classic dNBR confusion in heterogeneous canopies (Mediterranean pine, tropical eucalypt) by ~5 %.
algorithms.accuracy_band_cpu_fallback: When the GPU is unavailable, the dispatcher serves the dNBR-only path (drops the Prithvi probe). Accuracy degrades to ~0.72-0.80 (the classical dNBR baseline). The receipt's served_via.tier='cpu' surfaces this degradation; agents should treat the result as 'best available' rather than equivalent to the gpu tier.

inputs.explanation: Sentinel-2 NDVI at the cell. The optical view of biomass / vegetation density.

Sentinel-1 VV backscatter at the cell. The SAR view of structure / scattering / canopy. Tessera-style cross-modal contrastive pretraining (Barlow Twins on aligned S1+S2) is the published prior; we ship the pixel-wise consistency check as a cheap proxy that captures the same information.

algorithms.formula: let n = clamp((indices.ndvi + 1) / 2, 0, 1); let v = clamp((sentinel1_raw - (-25)) / 20, 0, 1); consistency_score = 1 - abs(n - v); inconsistency = abs(n - v)

output.explanation: Two outputs (consistency 0..1 and inconsistency 0..1, complementary). High inconsistency flags cells where the optical and SAR worlds disagree — typically a recent disturbance (clearcut: NDVI drops, VV stays high), wet soil under canopy (NDVI high, VV high), or industrial / metallic targets (NDVI low, VV very high).

algorithms.when_to_use: Cheap pre-filter for anomaly hunting when an agent doesn't yet know what kind of disturbance to look for. Cells with inconsistency > 0.4 are worth chaining to `multimodal_change_score@1` or `clay_temporal_change@1` for a typed verdict.
algorithms.citation: Tessera v1 (arXiv 2506.20380) trains a Barlow Twins objective on aligned S1+S2 pairs and uses cross-modal agreement as the contrastive signal. WV-Net (arXiv 2406.18765) uses the same pattern on S1-only WV chips. Both report the cross-modal disagreement as a useful disturbance prior. The L1 distance shipped here is the simplest sufficient statistic of that pretraining signal.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: 5-day cloud-permitting; both inputs share the cadence.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Editorial. The proxy isn't published with a dedicated benchmark; expect AUC ≈ 0.7 vs hand-labelled disturbance polygons across the LandTrendr / GFC suite. The value is in cheap screening, not classification.

inputs.explanation: 1024-D Clay v1.5 embedding evaluated at the centre cell and its 8 neighbours. A field boundary is where the embedding distance between two adjacent cells crosses a threshold — Clay's wavelength-conditioned ViT picks up texture / phenology / soil shifts that pure index thresholds miss.

algorithms.formula: let v0 = recall(clay_v1 @ cell); for n in neighbors_8(cell): d[n] = 1 - cos(v0, recall(clay_v1 @ n)); is_boundary = max(d) > tau_high AND median(d) < tau_low; (defaults tau_high=0.18, tau_low=0.10 — calibrated against FTW v1 / FracTAL parcel ground truth)

output.explanation: 1 when the cell sits on a parcel edge — a strong asymmetry between the strongest neighbour distance and the median. Pair with a closing morphological op over a 3×3 sliding window for clean polygons.

algorithms.when_to_use: Drawing field boundaries from S2 alone, no labelled training data. Useful when the user asks 'show me the parcel I'm standing on'. Output is per-cell; aggregate over a region for vector polygons.
algorithms._deterministic_note: Depends on the Clay checkpoint hash + neighbour cell layout; both are pinned in the receipt.
algorithms.citation: Tolan et al. (2024) Field-scale boundary delineation using foundation embeddings, NeurIPS Workshop on Tackling Climate Change. FracTAL / Delineate-Anything papers report similar boundary scores from contrastive ViT distance.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Per Sentinel-2 vintage (5–10 day cloud-permitting).
algorithms.accuracy_band: Editorial. Boundary IoU ≈ 0.55–0.70 vs FTW v1 polygons across mid-latitude croplands; lower in rainfed/sparse regions where parcel edges are textural rather than spectral.

inputs.explanation: Annual mean global horizontal irradiance (W/m²) from NASA POWER at the cell.
Elevation; combined with the 8 neighbours to derive slope and aspect via finite differences.
Total building footprint area in the cell. Multiplies the per-m² potential into a cell-level kWh/year ceiling.

algorithms.formula: slope = atan(|grad(z)|); aspect = atan2(dz/dx, dz/dy) measured clockwise from north; south_factor = max(0, cos(aspect - pi)); kwh_per_m2_yr = ghi * 8.76 * 0.18 * cos(slope) * (0.6 + 0.4 * south_factor); cell_kwh_yr = kwh_per_m2_yr * roof_area

output.explanation: Theoretical annual rooftop generation assuming 18 % module efficiency, slope-corrected insolation, and a 0.6+0.4·cos(aspect-south) orientation factor (south-pointing roofs get the full irradiance; north-pointing roofs ~60 %). Multiply by a 0.7–0.85 utilization factor for shading / azimuth diversity in a real deployment.

algorithms.when_to_use: Citywide solar siting and rooftop PV economic screening. Pairs with `solar_pv_likelihood@1` (existing detection) to find unused capacity.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall {bands: [power.allsky_sw, copdem30m.elevation_mean, overture.buildings.area_m2]} over the 9-cell neighborhood
algorithms.citation: Sengupta et al. (2018) NSRDB — National Solar Radiation Database; Hofierka & Suri (2002) on rooftop PV potential from DEM aspect.
algorithms.frequency_of_calculation: Annual; static after building footprints + DEM are seeded.
algorithms.accuracy_band: Theoretical ceiling — agrees with PVWatts within ±15 % on flat roofs; higher error on steep roofs because the DEM 30 m grain undersamples sub-cell roof pitch.

inputs.explanation: Prithvi-EO-2.0 1024-D embedding at the cell at the pre-event tslot (caller-supplied). Anchors the 'normal' baseline that change is measured against.
Prithvi-EO-2.0 embedding at the same cell at the post-event tslot. Cosine drop vs the pre-event vector is the damage signal.

algorithms.formula: delta = 1 - cos(vec_pre, vec_post); damage_class = if delta < 0.05 then 'no_damage' elif delta < 0.12 then 'minor' elif delta < 0.22 then 'moderate' else 'severe'; (thresholds calibrated against xBD validation chips with Prithvi 1024-D embeddings)

output.explanation: Per-cell qualitative damage class. The delta value itself is also signed under `derivation.args.delta` for callers that want a continuous score.

algorithms.when_to_use: Earthquake / hurricane / conflict post-event triage when an agent has a clean pre-event Prithvi vintage. Far better than NDVI delta because Prithvi was pretrained on change-from-context rather than chlorophyll change.
algorithms.primitive: POST /v1/recall_polygon with two `tslot` values and band:[prithvi_eo2]; compute cosine delta locally
